disclaimer: This work is a manifestation of pure planetary physical and sociological causality.

prologo:
The basement of the San Lorenzo Municipal Library did not house books, but the physical sediment of an era that refused to be forgotten. In the center of the chamber, buried under three meters of reinforced concrete and decades of static, rested the device of bismuth and coltan. To the archive bureaucrats, that object was a museum piece, a brass curiosity devoid of function; to the laws of thermodynamics, it was a lightning rod awaiting the storm of the 21st century.

The change was not an explosion, but a friction. When the saturation of 5G protocols and the directional beams of the urban wireless network penetrated the strata of the building, the bimetallic alloy entered into resonance. The artifact did not process the information; it absorbed it, turning the incessant noise of civilization into a kinetic load that tore through the geometry of the room. The air, ionized and dense, began to crystallize into fractals of pure causality.

Runa and Nilo were at the epicenter of that collapse. They were neither heroes nor researchers, but the human remnants of a routine afternoon, absorbed by the torsion of space-time before their nervous systems could register the danger. Now, anchored in a reality where matter remembered what man had erased, both observers understood the nature of their sentence: they had ceased to be inhabitants of the city to become the first cartographers of an invisible geology. Beneath their feet, the Earth's crust vibrated with the echo of electromagnetic tensions, tracing maps upon the skin of the world. The cartography of planetary resonance had just begun, written in the raw language of industrial collision and the unbearable weight of an existence that no longer belonged to linear time.

capitulos.sinopsis: Runa and Nilo materialize in the dense Sumatran jungle in 1883 after the portal's collapse, facing frequency fatigue and uncovering the threat of the Krakatoa volcano.
texto: The transition through the Acoustic Vacuum Vortex was not a gradual transit, but a traumatic laceration of reality that displaced Runa and Nilo's atomic mass from the sterile safety of the library into a dimension where time lacked a linear chronology. The sensation of being dismembered at a submolecular level preceded an absolute gravitational collapse; the pressure exerted on their ocular cavities and the sudden void in their diaphragms were instantly replaced by the viscous resistance of thick mud saturated with organic detritus. The silence of the library, saturated with technological static, was replaced by a dull roar that seemed to emanate from the very core of the Earth, a tectonic sound that shook their skeletons before depositing them violently onto the vegetal bed of a dense, primitive jungle.

The impact on the damp soil of Sumatra, in that tumultuous year of 1883, resulted in a dry thud that expelled the air from their lungs, forcing them to inhale a mixture saturated with spores, humidity, and the pungent aroma of vegetal decomposition. Around them, the vegetation was not the domesticated infrastructure of a modern city, but a jungle mass of thick stalks, tree ferns of cyclopean proportions, and a green twilight that seemed to devour every trace of direct sunlight. They were intruders in an age of steam and sweat, stripped of the protection of concrete walls, left exposed to the indomitable ferocity of an ecosystem that did not yet know the standardization of global telecommunications.

Frequency Decompression Fatigue struck their nervous systems with surgical brutality the moment they pushed themselves up from the mud. A sharp, metallic, and piercing hum settled into the ear canals of both—a residual frequency of the torsion that had dragged them through the ether and now refused to dissipate. Their hair, charged with electrical static that bristled every filament until it defied gravity, emitted small bluish sparks each time the movement of their limbs rubbed against the synthetic fibers of their clothing. That ionic charge was the physical proof of their alienation: their bodies were human batteries attempting to level their electrical potential with an atmosphere that had not yet been contaminated by the electromagnetic pollution of the coming century.

The pain radiating from their temples to the base of their skulls was an unequivocal symptom that the bimetallic resonance of the artifact still persisted in their tissues, acting as a beacon of residual energy. Nilo, his eyes bloodshot from the intracranial pressure, tried to stabilize his balance while the humming modulated its intensity, turning rhythmic, as if the planet itself were sending coded signals that his brain was not prepared to decode. The environment was not only hostile due to its climate, but because of the sheer instability of the electromagnetic energy emanating from their own pores, creating a bubble of interference that unsettled the jungle insects and caused nearby leaves to curl toward them, seeking the charge that the siblings, unintentionally, were radiating.

Nilo, with hands trembling but trained by years of technical obsession, extracted from his side the Aether Seismograph he still carried after the jump. The artifact, a piece of handcrafted engineering that combined bismuth gears with coltan filaments, showed signs of temporal trauma: its external structure was covered by a thin layer of metallic frost. Observing the interior through the transparent casing, he noted that the analog clockwork mechanism, normally fluid, had stopped in an absolute paralysis. The main needle, composed of a magnetic alloy, vibrated with violent tension before fixing itself in an immutable direction, pointing with sepulchral rigidity toward the magnetic north of an island that did not yet suspect the catastrophe looming over it.

The stopping of the gears was not a malfunction, but a symptom that the local magnetism of the Earth in the year 1883 possessed a purity and intensity the mechanism could not process without collapsing. The needle refused to oscillate, blocked by the power of the planetary magnetic field, which dictated the position of any object containing traces of metal from the modern era. Nilo understood, with an existential nausea, that the device had ceased to be a measurement tool to become a static anchor, a cursed compass pointing in the direction of the imminent end, reminding them that in this time, technology was not a servant, but a foreign body that nature was trying to expel.

Runa, maintaining a frigid composure despite the terror gnawing at her insides, searched her pocket for the touch screen of her mobile phone, hoping that consumer technology might somehow manage to calibrate their position in that tropical hell. Upon pressing the power button, the bluish light of the screen briefly illuminated the blackened mud, showing an interface desperately searching for a connection to nonexistent towers. There were no signals, no 4G protocols, not even the electromagnetic trace of a local network; the device only found the void, a data abyss where the absence of cell towers caused the processor to raise its power consumption to critical levels in a vain attempt to find a link to the 21st century.

Each second the screen remained lit, the battery percentage plummeted, drained by the frantic search for a network that would not be born for more than a century. Runa watched as the charge indicator descended with astonishing speed—a cruel metaphor for her own vitality in that prehistoric setting. It was a dying light in a world of organic shadows, a piece of silicon that, far from being a bridge to her home, was becoming a dead weight of metal and glass, consuming its last energy in a useless function while the jungle, impassive, watched the twilight of a civilization that had not yet been conceived there.

A tropical heatwave, dense and laden with a humidity that felt like a damp hand against their faces, finalized the confirmation of their geographical location. The air was not only hot, but carried a mineral essence, a smell of sulfur that clung to the throat and left, with every exhalation, a lingering taste of ash and volcanic eruptions. Runa recognized the atmospheric pattern: the air of the Sumatra coast in the Dutch East Indies, an atmosphere charged with geological premonitions. The atmospheric pressure was so high that oxygen seemed difficult to process, exacerbating the fatigue of their systems and reminding them that, in this era, nature was not a backdrop, but a tectonic force that breathed at the same rhythm as their hearts.

The dense sulfurous haze filtered through the trees, creating a yellowish mist that hindered visibility and tinted the horizon a copper, unnatural color. Heat radiated from the ground, warming the soles of their boots and sending thermal shockwaves toward their limbs, while the sounds of the local fauna, unusually nervous, composed a soundtrack of unsettling cacophonies. They were on the threshold of a cataclysm, surrounded by an atmosphere that was already processing the first gaseous emissions of Krakatoa—an air that announced the imminent disintegration of the island geography and the immensity of a volcanic fury that would change the globe's climate.

Evaluating the surrounding terrain, Runa felt the weight of the sticky mud on her worn boots, which threatened to suck their feet into the depths of a saturated floor. Despite the wear, the footwear demonstrated surprising resilience, protecting their feet from sharp roots and the hot sediments that formed the jungle's surface. Beside her, Nilo suffered a more visible transformation: his blue jacket, designed for the temperate and protected climates of urban libraries, began to saturate with a thick layer of ambient humidity. The synthetic fabric, unable to absorb so much condensation, became heavy, turning into a damp second skin that stole his body heat and reminded him that they were completely defenseless against the extreme meteorology of a century without climate infrastructure.

The jacket, now dark and glistening with water, hung from his shoulders like a shroud of synthetic material, an ironic reminder of technological modernity facing the organic immensity of the jungle. Nilo tried to shake off the excess moisture, but his movements were slow, hampered by the heaviness of his clothing and the exhaustion accumulated after the distortion. Runa watched him, noting how every fold of his clothes trapped the spores and volcanic dust that had already begun to descend like a fine, gray rain—a layer that would soon cover all the technology of their attire with the ash of the volcano that had not yet awakened, but was already claiming its right to bury them.

Runa, imposing a military discipline upon herself, dictated an emergency protocol intended to safeguard their only technological belongings from environmental degradation and power consumption. With quick, authoritative gestures, she ordered Nilo to place all electronic devices, from the phone to the components of the seismograph, inside the Faraday Isolation Pouch. The pouch, manufactured with a mesh of copper wires and graphene capable of blocking any electromagnetic field, was now their only sanctuary. The need to conserve energy to the maximum became vital; if the battery ran out before they understood the nature of their anchorage, they would be definitively disconnected from any possibility of return or communication, condemned to be ghosts in a chronology that did not belong to them.

Nilo, complying with the order with the precision of a technician, placed the devices into the case, feeling how the isolation blocked the electrical hum of the apparatuses. The technological silence that followed was overwhelming; as he sealed the pouch, an artificial peace descended upon them, but the disconnection from the metallic hum left them with a sensation of emptiness, as if an extension of their own bodies had been amputated. The energy of the outside world continued to vibrate, but their tools were protected, confined to the darkness of the Faraday mesh. Now, without the glow of the screen or the magnetic ticking of the seismograph, Runa and Nilo were reduced to their biological condition, two accidental observers forced to face the environment through sight, touch, and fear.

To obtain a strategic view, Nilo approached the massive trunk of a palm tree, whose dimensions surpassed any specimen in the botanical libraries, and began the ascent. His fingers sank into the rough, damp, and lichen-covered bark that smelled of ammonia. Every climb was a titanic effort that tested the endurance of his muscles, while Runa, from below, scanned the jungle with tense attention, alert to any movement that was not that of the wind. The height would allow Nilo to pass above the dense jungle canopy and obtain a clear horizon, a perspective they needed to understand the scope of the danger they felt pulsing beneath their feet.

Upon reaching the crown, Nilo froze, his breath caught in a dry throat. In the distance, breaking the line of the tropical horizon, the imposing silhouette of the Krakatoa volcano rose like an irate titan. A column of gray ash, vast and continuous, spewed from the crater, rising toward the stratosphere with an uninterrupted violence that obscured the sun and cast long, threatening shadows over the archipelago. It was not a silent eruption; it was the roar of the crust fracturing, a spectacle of geological energy that, even from a distance, made him feel the insignificance of their lives. The volcano was alive, and its breath of fire and stone marked the beginning of a cartography of destruction they were destined to witness, prisoners of the eternal echo of that cataclysm.

capitulos.sinopsis: The siblings encounter a native Sundanese gatherer and discover through a newspaper that they are but a few days away from the Krakatoa catastrophe.
texto: The vegetation of the archipelago unfurled before Runa and Nilo not as an organic landscape, but as a containment structure, a system of botanical gears that smothered any trace of the urban logic of San Lorenzo. The humidity was a thermal slab that saturated their lungs, as they advanced with difficulty over a floor carpeted in decomposing humus and roots that looked like exposed tendons. The silence of this jungle was not the absence of noise, but a dense acoustic pressure, a void that their instincts tried to fill with the memory of the static from the 5G towers. It was in that state of feverish alertness that the silhouette emerged from the foliage, a figure cinched in a loincloth of woven fibers, with a torso marked by the scars of resin harvesting and eyes lit with the vigilance of one who knows every pulse of their territory.

The Sundanese gatherer stopped, tensing a small bow of dark wood, observing the intruders with a curiosity devoid of immediate hospitality. Runa, feeling the electronic void of their devices, understood with an icy clarity that the tools of her time had perished in the spatial twist. The language of isolation was not a spoken tongue, but a choreography of minimal gestures, a low-level communication system that demanded surgical precision. With her hands raised to demonstrate the absence of weapons, Runa knelt, clearing the mud from the ground to turn the damp earth into a canvas where she began to trace elementary geometric diagrams, representing the setting sun, the location of the sea, and the absolute loss of their origin through crude but eloquent strokes.

The native remained motionless, watching Runa's drawings with an atavistic skepticism. His eyes, deep as pits of tar, soon shifted from the earth to rest upon the attire of the newcomers. Nilo made the mistake of moving, and the metallic gleam of a nickel-alloy zipper caught the light filtered through the forest canopy. The gatherer took a step forward, with an almost physical wariness, extending a calloused finger to touch the jagged edge of the industrial fastener and the injected-polymer buttons. To that man, whose technology was limited to stone, bamboo, and fire-forged steel, those synthetic rivets were excrescences of an incomprehensible witchcraft, anomalies that defied the purity of the matter he manipulated day by day in his labor.

Runa, interpreting the gatherer's fear, offered a gesture of peace, allowing the man to examine the smooth, unnatural surface of their clothes. There was a total disconnection between the worlds: on one side, artificial silk and plastics, descendants of a chemical industry that had not yet conceived the disaster that its existence provoked; on the other, the rawness of survival on an island that would soon cease to be habitable. The native, after confirming that those objects emanated neither heat nor poison, softened his posture, though the trace of suspicion remained in his gaze. With a mute sign, he indicated for the siblings to follow him, leading them through a network of paths that snaked toward the gloom of a clearing, where the smoke from a solitary hearth rose in vertical columns.

The gatherer's camp was an extension of the surrounding geology, an improvised refuge where nature and the human hand merged in a precarious harmony. While the man rummaged through his sparse belongings to offer some comfort, Runa spotted a sheet of yellowed paper, nearly disintegrated by the tropical humidity, which served as protection against the damp earth on one of the bamboo platforms. It was a Dutch colonial newspaper, a fragment of an administration that still believed itself the master of the tides. As she brushed away the layer of dust with trembling fingers, the year emerged with the cruelty of a sentence: August 1883. The weight of the date fell upon Runa's shoulders like a block of lead, invalidating all her temporal certainties.

The horror began to articulate itself in Runa's mind as the data from her school geography studies, forgotten in the classrooms of San Lorenzo, rushed to the surface. The proximity of Krakatoa was not just a historical fact, it was a physical condemnation that pulsed on the horizon. She knew the records of the catastrophe that was about to occur, the volcanic fury that would change the chemical composition of the global atmosphere and rewrite climate maps. They were situated in an imminent impact zone, a stage of annihilation where time was not a cycle, but a countdown initiated by the collision of their modern devices with a reality that was not prepared for their load.

As the revelation sank into Runa's nervous system, a micro-seism ran through the spine of the island, a subtle but tectonically unbearable vibration that caused the roots beneath their feet to writhe like dying snakes. The birds, which until then had sung a monotonous tune, rose in unison in a black frenzy, a coordinated surge toward the north, fleeing a premonition that only they could feel in the sulfur-laden air. Small mammals abandoned their burrows with the haste of one who recognizes dissonance before the event materializes, leaving the humans mired in a paralysis that emanated from the very earth itself.

In that instant, the astrolabe that Nilo kept in his pocket, a remnant of their original technical equipment that had survived the collapse, emitted a sympathetic vibration. It was barely a fraction of a second, a buzz of metal against metal, a mechanical reaction to the seismic wave rippling through the oceanic plate. The artifact was reacting to the kinetic energy of the earth, becoming a crude seismograph, an instrument capable of reading planetary tension before the crust fractured for good. Nilo, noticing the heat on his thigh, understood that the metal he carried was not merely an ornament, but a node of the same network that had transported them there, a magnetic bridge between the immutable past and the shattered future.

The gatherer, oblivious to the technical complexity of the revelation but deeply aware of the urgency of the tectonic movement, cast a look charged with primitive compassion toward the two siblings. He understood that, although foreigners, they were creatures lost in the chaos of the elements. Without further ado, he gestured with an urgency that admitted no debate, leading them toward his elevated hut, a structure built on pillars of ironwood and roofed with palm leaves. There, on the floor of interwoven bamboo, the three took refuge while outside, the world began to shake with the spasms preceding the great volcanic discharge.

The hut, though precarious, offered a temporary shelter against the cataclysm being announced on the horizon. Inside that suspended dwelling, the silence was absolute, broken only by Runa's ragged breathing and the residual hum of Nilo's astrolabe. The language of isolation had closed over them: they were no longer accidental observers, but witnesses to the imminence of a disaster that no modern technology could have foreseen. Beneath the structure, the earth continued to emit its muffled language, a tongue of stone and magma that reminded them that, at that point in history, they were the only cartographers of an imminent and apocalyptic disappearance.

capitulos.sinopsis: Nilo accidentally experiments with his smartphone's flashlight and discovers that microwaves excite the bismuth gears of the astrolabe.
texto: The night over the 1883 jungle loomed with a mineral density, an absolute darkness that seemed to possess mass of its own, sucking the starlight away before it could touch the arboreal canopy. There was no constant hum in that remote place, that low-frequency electric whisper that defines urban existence; the air was free from the pollution of transformers, from the static of high-tension wires, and from the invisible tide of data that, in the future, would bathe every atom on the planet. In that electromagnetic vacuum, the silence was a solid, heavy, and pure substance, a realm where the sound of an insect or the crack of a dry branch took on geological significance.

Beneath the vault of the cabin, built with native woods that still exuded sap, Runa slept with the rhythmic breathing of one who has accepted exile in the past. The setting was an immaculate canvas of natural history, oblivious to the wireless network that, ironically, would become the key to her return or the instrument of her disintegration. The absence of artificial disturbances conferred an unnatural calm upon the environment, a serenity that, for Nilo, translated into a suffocating urgency to unravel the anomaly that rested, inert and cold, in his leather satchel.

Driven by a curiosity bordering on the pathological, Nilo slipped out from under his blanket, feeling the dawn chill prickle his skin. With movements calculated not to alter the balance of the structure, he extracted the Aether Seismograph, that artifact cataloged as an analog brass piece that now weighed in his hands like a block of stellar lead. He observed the bismuth and coltan alloy in the gloom, running his fingers over the grooves of the metal. It was dead, thermally neutral, devoid of the vibration he had felt in the Library's sub-levels, behaving now like a simple relic from another century, indifferent to the law of causality that had brought him there.

The metal was a compact mystery, a geometry of gear teeth and bismuth spheres that seemed to have been forged in an industrial nightmare workshop. Nilo sought some sign, an indication that the machine was not an ornament but a latent quantum processor waiting for the right fuel. The surface, though visually complex, revealed no friction, no internal oscillation; it was the embodiment of metallic silence, an object guarding its secrets with the stubbornness of pure matter.

To examine the worn dials in detail, Nilo retrieved his smartphone, a device that no longer served as a communication tool, but as a technological relic whose battery was a resource as precious as water. His fingers trembled as he turned on the screen, whose whitish luminosity cut the profile of the cabin like a spectral ray of sun in the middle of the primitive night. With a nervous pulse, he activated the built-in flashlight; the LED began to emit a flow of microwaves and high-energy photons, a concentrated beam that cut through the darkness and projected directly onto the bismuth matrix.

The effect was immediate, a transgression of physical logic. The moment the smartphone's beam hit the crystallized alloys, the air in the cabin seemed to ionize, generating an acrid smell of ozone and overheated metal. The gears, which just a second before were part of an inert sculpture, emitted three mechanical turns, dry and violent, with a watchmaker's precision that cut the air like a knife. It was a metallic click—a clack, clack, clack—that resonated with the force of a gunshot, a series of kinetic interactions that defied inertia.

Runa sat up abruptly, eyes bloodshot with the adrenaline of a sudden awakening, her survival instinct triggered by the sound of the mechanism. Her eyes fixed first on the artificial light of the phone and then on the artifact, which was still vibrating with a harmonic frequency, dissipating a faint trail of radiant heat. Runa's look was a lightning bolt of contained fury; she knew well that every percentage of the device's battery was a critical reserve, an umbilical cord to the 21st century that they must not squander on erratic experiments.

"What are you doing, Nilo?" she hissed, ignoring the wonder the strange dance of the gears caused her to focus on her brother's tactical imprudence. "You're draining our only asset for a collector's curiosity. That device is not a toy, and this century has no outlets where you can recharge your toy. If the battery dies, we die in this era without a compass or salvation."

Nilo lowered the phone, but did not turn off the light, keeping the beam on the astrolabe while the tension in the cabin became unbearable. "Look at the gears, Runa. It's not manual friction. The metal has responded to the frequency, to the LED's emission spectrum. It's not the bulb's heat, it's the electromagnetic noise, the microwaves. This astrolabe was not designed for analog mechanics; it was designed to devour the radiation of the digital age that didn't even exist when it was built."

Sitting in the gloom, the debate erupted into whispers charged with raw analytical urgency. Both siblings, trapped in a wild environment, began to dissect the impossible with the coldness of desperate engineers. They watched how the surface of the bismuth, after the brief exposure, showed small geometric fractures that were not there before, as if the metal were reconfiguring its internal structure at a nanometric scale. Each flash of the screen seemed to endow the object with a synthetic life, a form of existence that fed directly on the remnants of their own technology.

Runa, with her hand extended but without touching the device, theorized with icy lucidity that the astrolabe was an analog antenna with an atavistic hunger. It was not a simple navigation tool; it was a quantum parasite. Her hypothesis was risky: the object operated as a transducer that converted modern electromagnetic emissions into kinetic energy, a mechanism that attempted to replicate the environment of its origin to feed a processing core that remained in a state of standby, a cryogenic dream waiting for the spark of the global network.

Nilo nodded, understanding the magnitude of the condemnation. They were charging a weapon that ran on the remains of the future, a machine that became heavier, denser, and more complex as it absorbed the energy of their device. The phone, now hot and emitting a subtle hum, was the bridge. The light was not just light; it was pure information, a signal that the artifact translated into turns, into changes of state, into a hidden cartography that began to be traced in the air through tiny electrical arcs.

The conversation was interrupted by an underground roar, a low-frequency vibration that was born in the bowels of the earth and propagated through the bamboo supports of the cabin. It was not a conventional earthquake; it was a structural creak, a spasm of the planetary crust that seemed to respond, with terrifying synchronicity, to the operation of the astrolabe. The cabin groaned, the woods strained, and the packed-earth floor rippled briefly, reminding them that geology was not a passive entity, but a living system that had begun to react to the activation of the object.

The air became heavy, saturated with a static charge that made the hair on their arms stand on end and caused the suspended dust to float in fractal patterns. Nilo finally turned off the flashlight, but the physical reaction did not cease; the artifact continued to emit a low, almost imperceptible hum that harmonized with the deep pulse of the earth. They looked at each other, understanding that the first step of their cartography of resonance was not a process of observation, but a declaration of war against the stability of the fabric surrounding them, a collision between linear time and the world's magnetic memory.

capitulos.sinopsis: The children spy on a camp of Dutch scientists and compare their rudimentary analog seismographs with the diamagnetic device.
texto: Dawn over the coast did not manifest with the transparency characteristic of past days, but through an oppressive sieve of volcanic particles floating in the lower stratosphere. Sunlight, attempting to pierce the dense layer of suspended ash, fragmented into long wavelengths, bathing the horizon in an unnatural chromatic palette; an electric violet mingled with the vermilion of dried blood, creating an atmosphere that distorted the perception of distance. Runa watched the glare fall onto the leaves of the trees like thick oil, staining the world with an apocalyptic urgency that made the fragility of the crust beneath her feet tangible.

Nilo, dragging his boots through volcanic sand that still held a residual heat, noticed how the air turned metallic, almost electric, as they neared the tide line. Visibility was a relative concept in that scenario where the sun resembled a coin of oxidized copper sinking into a sea of lead. The atmosphere was not only visually distorted but acoustically dead; the silence reigning on the coast was charged with a static pressure that raised the hair on their arms, constantly reminding them that physical reality had been altered by the municipal subsoil fissure.

A few meters from the breakers, where vegetation became wild and unruly due to the accumulation of minerals in the soil, the path opened into an esplanade dominated by an intrusive structure. It was a scientific camp of Dutch colonial manufacture, a series of waxed-canvas tents and wrought-iron frames that rose against the horizon like an anachronistic fortification. The surveyors had moved their heavy machinery to this remote enclave, ignoring that the ground they walked upon was nothing more than an unstable crust over a core that was beginning to sing in frequencies imperceptible to the human ear.

The camp's configuration denoted a methodological rigidity typical of the 19th century, with theodolites mounted on treated-wood tripods and heavy leather-lined transport crates. Runa identified the expeditioners' origin by the faded emblem of a Netherlands science academy, stamped with a bureaucratic precision that contrasted violently with the chaos of the surroundings. The men moved among the instruments with a parsimony that seemed an insult in the face of the impending geological fracture, not suspecting that every step they took on the earth was being recorded by the vibrations of the device Nilo hid in his clothes.

Crouched beneath the shadow cast by giant ferns, whose rough-textured leaves and purple veins acted as natural camouflage, the siblings approached the edge of the perimeter. Their bodies, accustomed to the concrete and wire environment of the city, felt strange in that virgin jungle, but their technical curiosity prevailed over fear. From their position, they could see the work tables arranged in the open air, covered in cartographic plans printed on vegetable parchment and measuring tools that looked like museum pieces rescued from oblivion.

The instrumentation deployed on the oak planks was a display of mechanical precision: mercury barometers that oscillated with hypnotic slowness and analog seismographs that used graphite nibs to trace meandering lines on drums of graph paper. For Runa, that spectacle was a collision of eras; she watched as the scientists adjusted the pendulums with almost religious attention, ignoring the magnetic fluctuations emanating from their own tools. It was a technology founded on weight, gravity, and contact physics, diametrically opposed to the diamagnetic artifact pulsating in Nilo's pocket.

Nilo, with his gaze fixed on a pendulum seismograph, compared the crudeness of the system to the molecular elegance of the bismuth and coltan device they guarded. The Dutch ingenuity relied on physical inertia, a suspended lead mass to register shock waves, while their own artifact operated at the level of spacetime torsion, reacting to causality before the seismic event even occurred. The miniaturization of their present, with its nanolithography circuits, felt now like a fragile toy compared to the imposing and tangible reliability of those gear-and-counterweight mechanisms that, despite their rusticity, did not lie about the subsoil pressure.

The Dutch scientists, men with faces weathered by salt spray and thick beards, argued vehemently around a seismic map spread over a wooden crate. Runa could catch fragments of their conversation; with an arrogance based on statistical data and historical records, the researchers maintained that the archipelago's volcanic activity was receding after the minor bursts recorded in May. Their confidence lay in the linearity of geological history: if the pattern of previous eruptions had ceased, the future could only be calm—a technical deduction that completely ignored the magnetic mutation occurring beneath the seabed.

The men pointed with ink- and coal-stained index fingers at the peaks of their seismograph charts, dismissing any anomaly as mere static noise or mechanical instrument failure. They did not see the erratic oscillations of the needles as a warning, but as a calibration error they rushed to correct by adjusting bronze screws. The scientists' ignorance granted them a temporary peace, a collective blindness that Runa found deeply disturbing; it was the calm of those who believe they can master nature through measurement, without understanding that nature had begun to speak in a language their paper needles did not know how to transcribe.

However, Runa noticed a detail that chilled her blood: the recording needles of the Dutch seismographs, although apparently random in their strokes, were beginning to synchronize. Every time the bismuth and coltan core in Nilo's pocket emitted an imperceptible pulse, the heavy mechanical needles of the seismographs reacted with a rhythmic jolt, a subtle echo that transformed the scribbled paper line into a recognizable geometric pattern. They were not tectonic tremors that the Dutch were registering, but the echo of the resonance that Runa and Nilo carried with them; the artifact was communicating with the surrounding matter, dictating the pulse to the decimononical tools.

Nilo felt the weight of the device at his side, an intense heat beginning to radiate through the fabric of his jacket. The magnetic induction caused by the proximity of the iron masses in the Dutch equipment was creating a feedback field; the seismograph magnets, designed to detect terrestrial magnetism, were being oversaturated by the exotic energy of the bimetallic core. The artifact was not just emitting signals, but was suctioning the magnetism from the tools around it, seeking a thermodynamic equilibrium that the Dutch equipment was not designed to withstand.

The device's temperature rose to the point of discomfort, a dry heat that seemed to emanate from the very core of the Earth. Nilo, worried about the object's integrity, tried to hide it even further, but the low-frequency hum the artifact emitted began to be audible—a sound similar to the rubbing of two crystals under pressure that began to seep through the camp's silence. The scientists, distracted by their tables, had not yet noticed, but the air around them was becoming charged with ozone, an unmistakable sign that local ionization was reaching critical levels.

In that moment of static tension, a guard dog resting under the main map table stood up abruptly. The animal, a mongrel with coarse fur and glassy eyes, raised its snout toward the area where the children were hiding, its ears catching the resonant frequency escaping from Nilo's device. The dog let out a sharp bark, a sound that cut the air like a gunshot, and began to growl toward the ferns, the hair on its spine bristling from the static charge emanating from the siblings' intrusion into that space of scientific order.

The animal's sound immediately attracted the attention of the surveyors, who abandoned their instruments with a speed denoting years of vigilance in hostile environments. One of the men grabbed an oil lantern from a stand, while another began to unbuckle the holster of a heavy expedition carbine. The atmosphere of scientific analysis dissipated instantly, replaced by territorial instinct and fear of the unknown; the camp, which until a few seconds ago was a bastion of human reason, transformed into a nest of suspicion and hostility.

Runa and Nilo did not wait to be discovered. With the agility of those who have spent their childhood dodging authority, they plunged into the thickness of the woods, breaking branches and moving across terrain that, beneath their feet, seemed to vibrate in response to the disorder they left behind. As they fled, they could hear the scientists' shouts and the dog's barking, which drifted away but no longer mattered as much as the certainty that their presence in the world was already an uncontrollable variable. The device in Nilo's pocket suddenly cooled, leaving a thermal void that contrasted with the adrenaline of their escape, marking the end of their first encounter with Old World science and the beginning of their definitive exile into the cartography of the invisible.

capitulos.titulo: Chapter 5: The Geometry of the Jungle
capitulos.sinopsis: Compasses go haywire due to volcanic magnetism; Runa explains the properties of piezoelectric coltan while they sketch a physical map.
texto: The atmosphere on the island had ceased to be breathable air, transforming into a dense fluid charged with a static that made the skin crawl and distorted the perception of distance. With every step, the magnetic compasses Runa carried on her harness spiraled into mechanical madness; the induction needles, designed to align with the geomagnetic poles, spun frantically on their axes, betrayed by the volcanic magnetism emanating from the basaltic depths. The anomaly, fueled by the remains of coltan buried beneath the soil, turned the local magnetic field into a labyrinth of erratic vectors that rendered all conventional navigation instruments useless, reducing precision technology to mere useless ornamentation.

Runa, her gaze hardened by the necessity of survival, dismissed the digital devices with a disdainful gesture, forcing herself to resort to orientation techniques of archaic simplicity. She stopped before a centuries-old trunk, whose faces less exposed to direct solar radiation exhibited a thick, velvety layer of moss—a biological compass dictated by the persistence of humidity. Where steel failed against the turbulence of the ether, biology responded with an immutable constancy, allowing her to trace a tentative heading toward the jungle interior, guided solely by the geometric arrangement of the flora in the face of magnetic north's disorientation.

Beneath her boots, the ground transformed into a sponge of pumice, a vast expanse of porous volcanic deposits that crunched under the weight of their bodies with a dry, almost mineral sound. This low-density crust, the product of an eruption that aeons ago petrified the volcano's breath, acted as a trap for moisture, absorbing every drop of condensation into an alveolar structure that gave the surface a deceptive instability. Nilo, stumbling at the ground's inconsistency, felt the terrain give way beneath his weight, forcing him to readjust his balance at every moment to avoid being trapped in the rocky bed.

Runa, conscious of the physical danger the environment posed to the musculoskeletal system, validated the importance of her technical exploration footwear, designed with a high-density polymer sole capable of absorbing the uneven friction of the pumice. The boots, equipped with reinforced lateral stabilizers, prevented Runa's ankles from suffering mechanical sprains in the crevices that, like open maws, extended across the ground. Each movement was calculated with near-robotic precision, minimizing the impact on a surface that, while soft to the touch, hid obsidian edges capable of tearing through any fabric without adequate protection.

As they walked, Runa pointed to a mineral outcrop erupting from the ground like an oxidized, steel-colored scar; it was crystallized coltan, the heart of their misfortune and the key to their cartography. In a technical, dispassionate tone, Runa explained to Nilo how this matter did not merely store energy, but reacted violently to variations in tectonic pressure, behaving analogously to the auditory compression experienced during an abrupt change in altitude. The coltan's crystalline network, under the tension of the continental plates shifting beneath their feet, compressed and released piezoelectric pulses, converting telluric vibration into an electromagnetic signal that altered the very structure of the surrounding air.

The comparison was as accurate as it was terrifying: just as the human ear suffers from a lack of compensation for atmospheric pressure, the material reality in that sector of the island collapsed under the excess of electric potential. Nilo listened, observing how his sister's explanation was not a theoretical abstraction, but a description of the laws of physics beginning to fracture before them. The pressure of the subterranean layers, pressing against the veins of coltan, generated an invisible symphony that affected not only the compasses but the coherence of solid matter—a resonance phenomenon that made the air vibrate at the same frequency as glass about to shatter under a high-pitched sound.

As they moved toward the heart of the forest, they found specimens of local flora, plants with broad, leathery leaves that, far from being purely organic, accumulated a fine film of gray mineral dust on their surfaces. That sediment, laden with metallic particles from the volcano, emitted subtle flashes under the dim light, an electrostatic luminescence provoked by the friction of the charged air flowing through the leaf veins. The plant, in its process of photosynthesis, seemed to have integrated the residual magnetism into its metabolism, becoming passive receptors that glowed with the intensity of a contained storm.

Nilo, driven by reckless curiosity, brought the chassis of the astrolabe—the artifact recovered from the subsoil—near one of those shimmering leaves. In the precise instant the device's metal grazed the vegetal surface, a voltaic arc of static micro-discharge leapt between the two points, ionizing the air with the acrid smell of burnt ozone. The shock was instantaneous; the energy accumulated in the artifact was released in a dry crackle that violently and unnaturally caused Nilo's blond hair to stand on end, leaving it suspended in the air as if drawn by an invisible centrifugal force.

The young man's bewilderment was interrupted by the immediate reaction of Runa, who, aware of the danger of a static feedback that could destroy the artifact or stop their hearts, lunged toward him. With mechanical promptness, she stripped Nilo of the astrolabe and urgently placed it inside a thick leather satchel, internally lined with synthetic polymer insulating cloths. The gesture was a containment maneuver; the leather acted as a natural dielectric, severing the connection between the artifact and the forest's ambient charge, thus halting the destructive feedback that threatened to turn that place into a point of catastrophic discharge.

The silence that followed the object's containment was soon replaced by the constant, incessant roar coming from the Sunda Strait, an oceanic din that echoed against the cliffs like a war drum. The sound of the water, agitated by deep currents affected by volcanic activity, blended with the acoustic exhalations of the main crater, a gaseous hiss that emerged from the fumaroles with the regularity of an animal's breath. It was a symphony of geology in motion, a reminder that the island was not a static terrain, but a platform in constant tension, where sea and fire dictated the tempo of existence.

Runa, seeking to establish order amidst the chaos, pulled a physical notebook from her gear, a tome bound in carbon fiber whose pages were resistant to humidity and mineral erosion. With the calm of a cartographer drawing a forbidden map, she began to sketch geometric schemes of the island, relying on the historical documentation she kept in her memory as a security archive. She was not tracing a physical geography, but a network of resonances, marking the points where the crystallized coltan interacted with the volcano's magnetism, creating an invisible topography that only the eyes of the artifact—now isolated—could understand in the long term.

Her lines, precise and technical, transformed the wild jungle into a series of vector coordinates, connecting the detected anomalies to the island's deep structure. Each line drawn on paper was an attempt to domesticate geological causality, to understand the energy flows that had dragged the siblings to this point of no return. While Nilo regained his composure, Runa kept writing, building a mental map that sought not an exit, but an understanding of the map of planetary resonance, the only possible guide in a world where matter, finally, had begun to claim its own language.

capitulos.sinopsis: Upon reaching a coastal town, they exchange metal clips for supplies and calculate that thirty-six hours remain until the magmatic collapse.
texto: The horizon fragmented into a line of grayish mist as Runa and Nilo crested the final ridge of the coastal hill, revealing the Strait settlement. It was a conglomerate of precarious structures, built from shipwrecks, salt-treated timber, and sheets of rusted zinc that groaned under the constant lashing of the sea wind. The native fishermen, their faces weathered by the sun and the elements, stood stationed on the worm-eaten wooden piers, maintaining a static vigil over the waters with an intensity bordering on religious devotion. Their eyes did not search for schools of fish, but rather sank into the disturbances on the surface, where the tides displayed erratic and unnatural behavior, with currents flowing at right angles and whirlpools that, instead of spinning, seemed to contract upon themselves.

The atmosphere in the camp was a taut canvas of resignation and contained terror. The local men and women walked with cautious steps, as if they feared the ground, saturated with a telluric vibration that was just beginning to manifest, could open under their weight at any moment. The vessels, small fiberglass boats and reinforced rowboats, had been hauled inland with an urgency that betrayed an atavic instinct, a silent understanding that the sea was ceasing to be their sustenance and becoming the epicenter of a devastating geological phenomenon. The town's children, unusually quiet, hid beneath the fishing nets, watching Runa and Nilo with the suspicion reserved for omens arriving from the mainland.

Beyond the pier, the Sunda Strait had been transformed into a cauldron of suspended sediments. The water, normally crystal clear, presented a leaden and murky coloration, laden with a mineral suspension of volcanic ash and minerals eroded by deep tectonic friction. Over this opaque surface floated extensive rafts of pumice, a porous and brittle carpet that stretched as far as the eye could see, blocking the progress of any smaller vessel. Each time a wave collided with this floating sediment, a dull click was produced, a metallic resonance suggesting that the composition of the volcanic rock was altered by unusual exposure to high-energy fields emerging from the fracture in the seabed.

Nilo stopped walking, feeling a sudden void in his balance. Inside his backpack, the bismuth astrolabe began to emit an ultrasonic oscillation, a frequency so sharp it escaped the human auditory spectrum, yet resonated with devastating power in the observers' cranial cavities. The environment's response was instantaneous and terrifying: a flock of seabirds resting on the adjacent cliffs fell into an erratic frenzy, abandoning their nests in an explosion of wings and dissonant squawks. The birds spun in perfect circles, disoriented by the electromagnetic disturbance emitted by the artifact, until, one by one, they began to plunge into the water in a display of biological collision, unable to navigate the new geometry of the air.

Nilo adjusted his gear strap, feeling the heat emanating from the astrolabe's metal through the canvas. He understood, with an existential chill, that the device was not only measuring the instability but amplifying it, acting as a beacon for the forces gathering beneath the crust. The fishermen noticed the disturbance and backed away, pointing at the apparatus with trembling hands. To the natives, the collapse of the birds' flight was a divine sentence, while to Nilo, it was simply evidence that the planet's resonance lines were being short-circuited. The air, previously static, now felt heavy, as if every molecule of oxygen had been replaced by a charged particle that refused to allow light to pass normally.

When they attempted to approach the nearest trading post, a mahogany wood shed where a local merchant oversaw the distribution of supplies, Runa tried to use a 21st-century metal coin as a means of exchange. The object, shiny and coated with a modern alloy, proved to be an aberration to the elderly merchant. He held the coin between his dirty fingers, rotating it under the twilight light with an expression of instinctive rejection. It was not just the lack of commercial value that prevented him from accepting it; it was the very nature of the material, the coin's strange ability to reflect light in a way that seemed to absorb it, which provoked in him a repulsion bordering on superstition. The coin was returned with a dry disdain, confirming that the technology of their era of origin lacked context in this distorted present.

Runa, without losing her composure, analyzed the elder's improvised inventory: dried fruit, salted meat, and wineskins of fresh water. Her gaze fell upon a simple metal paperclip holding a bundle of technical notes in her pocket, along with a high-resistance elastic band she used to organize cables in the library's underworld. With surgical precision, she presented both objects. The merchant, intrigued by the flexibility and tenacity of the elastic—a material non-existent in his limited ecology—and by the purity of the paperclip's steel, nodded slowly. The exchange was an exercise in elemental pragmatism: the disposable industrial technology of a lost time became the most precious treasure for a world struggling to understand physical scarcity.

While the merchant prepared the provision package, Runa took the opportunity to study the position of the sun relative to the rock formations bordering the coast. Using her own fingers as a sextant, she traced angles of elevation and compared the inclination of the solar beam with the barometric pressure data that Nilo's astrolabe projected in his internal memory. After a few minutes of frantic mental calculations, her face turned pale. The rate of drift of the local magnetic axis was exponential; if the collision rhythm of the plates beneath the strait remained constant, tectonic stability would shatter completely. The verdict was final: exactly thirty-six hours remained before the crater's great collapse phase initiated its final chain of destruction.

The environment in the camp changed suddenly. The density of negative ions in the air reached a critical point, causing the hair on Runa and Nilo's arms to stand up simultaneously. A tingling sensation, similar to a constant static discharge, began to crawl across their exposed skin, as if the oxygen itself were attempting to strip them of their electrical charge. The fishermen began to gather their nets with clumsy gestures, affected by the same electrical discomfort that saturated the surroundings. The metal of their tools began to vibrate with a low note, an industrial litany that announced the awakening of the lower strata, preparing the earth for an event of geological magnitude.

Runa took the supplies and turned to Nilo with the urgency dictated by the numbers. The tactical decision was clear: they could not remain on the coast, where the gas accumulation and the imminent volcanic tsunami would wipe them out of existence. They had to gain altitude. She pointed toward the basaltic slopes of the neighboring island, a craggy volcanic formation that protruded from the water like an obsidian tooth. That was the goal. The altitude would allow them not only to escape the imminent collapse but to locate the exact focus of the static energy induction, the point where the Earth's crust collided with the remnants of their own crashed technology.

The ascent began under a twilight light that seemed to filter the visible spectrum, lending the rocks an iridescent glow. Each step on the basalt was a challenge against gravity, while the tingling in their skin intensified, transforming into a constant prick in their nerve endings. As they climbed, the sound of the sea faded away, replaced by the hissing of gas emerging from small fissures on the slope. It was a march into the heart of chaos, a pilgrimage of cartographers forced to record the end of a world before time itself was recruited by the forces they had unleashed themselves. At the summit, the magnitude of the planetary resonance awaited them, hiding beneath the basalt the secret of a matter that refused to be forgotten.

capitulos.titulo: Chapter 7: The Ascension of Electromagnetic Silence
capitulos.sinopsis: Climbing the volcanic ridges, they realize their bimodal physical proximity is necessary to prevent the gears from freezing.
texto: The ascent toward the volcanic ridges of San Lorenzo felt not like a geographic climb, but an incursion into a tectonic fabric wounded by fatigue. Beneath Runa and Nilo’s boots, the earth's crust, usually solid, exhibited the porosity of a calcined sponge. The terrain, altered by an incessant microseismic activity that made the hair on their necks stand on end, gave way under the weight of their steps in stretches of friable sediment. Volcanic rocks, black and sharp as shards of obsidian glass, crunched under an invisible pressure, releasing the acrid smell of sulfur and oxidized metal that saturated every inhalation, reminding them that the ground they trod was, in essence, a plate charged with a latent electrical energy struggling to break free.

Each upward movement demanded a choreographic coordination, a precise dance atop the crumbling remnants of the hill. Runa, leading the way, scanned for the densest points of support, avoiding the fissures that distilled a bluish static, a clear sign that the zone’s electromagnetic charge not only inhabited the artifact they carried but radiated from the very bowels of the mountain. The landscape, a dystopia of fractured strata and long shadows, twisted before their eyes, creating an illusion of continuous movement where reality seemed to fray at the edges of their vision, forcing them to advance with the caution of those walking upon glass about to shatter.

While navigating a ravine where the stone had been pulverized into a fine conductive sand, Runa paused, her voice cutting through the sibilant silence of the wind. With fingers that barely grazed the surface of the artifact, she began to explain to Nilo the technical distinction that dictated their new existence. Conventional electric current, she said with the coldness of an engineer, was a directed and domesticated flow, a channel of confined electrons; however, the piezoelectric properties of the bismuth and coltan alloy operated under a logic of molecular torsion. The alloy did not conduct energy but forced it to crystallize into physical tensions, transforming the invisible vibration of radio waves into mechanical torque capable of altering matter itself.

Nilo observed with fascination the way the bismuth seemed to absorb the ambient light, refracting it not into colors, but into a geometric darkness that suggested density. For Runa, the bismuth was not a metal but a mediator: a catalyst that converted the electronic noise of the 21st century into a living gear. Each gram of that alloy was a memory of tension, a crystalline structure that expanded and contracted to the rhythm of the industrial pollution emanating from the distant city. It was not a battery, nor a circuit, but a transdimensional bridge built with the atomic precision of a geological coincidence that neither of them fully understood, yet whose laws they were beginning to master.

At a bend in the path, the instinct for survival compelled an urgent technical inspection. Runa pulled her terminal from her bag, observing the screen with a mixture of repulsion and need. The charge percentage held at 88%, an anomalous figure given the duration of exposure, which confirmed her theory regarding the device’s selective drain toward the central artifact. Beside her, Nilo checked his own terminal; the digital display flickered with erratic instability, showing 74% and dropping before his eyes at an imperceptible yet inexorable speed, feeding the gears of the brass mechanism they carried on their backs.

The dissonance between both devices was the first map of their cartography of resonance. While Runa’s terminal seemed to act as an equilibrium emitter, Nilo’s behaved as a drain vector, yielding its energetic potential to stabilize the artifact’s frequency. This misalignment was not trivial; it was physical evidence that the electromagnetic load was distributed unevenly across their bodies, turning them into living poles of an open circuit. They put away the devices, conscious that any extra fluctuation in the local phone network could alter the delicate energy flow keeping the artifact in a state of operational latency.

To mitigate the energy bleed, they verified once more the integrity of the tanned leather bag in which they transported the mechanism. The leather, treated with a dense saline solution, served as a rudimentary yet effective Faraday cage, designed to isolate the artifact from external interference and, more importantly, to prevent the antennas of their own phones from searching for a signal in the electromagnetic vacuum of the hill. Upon sealing the brass buckles, the silence inside the bag became absolute, a sensory void that protected the delicate balance of the alloy against radio pulses that would have otherwise triggered an uncontrollable chain reaction in the bismuth.

The isolation of the leather was not only mechanical but psychological; as they closed the compartment, they felt the pressure on their shoulders stabilize. However, the effectiveness of the insulation was precarious. The structure of the bag was designed to absorb the residual static of their movements, filtering electromagnetic waves as a sieve filters sand. Each reinforced seam, each fold of the leather, worked as a shield against the exterior, keeping the bismuth core in a bubble of isolation necessary to prevent local reality from fracturing under the weight of an excess of data the artifact was not prepared to process.

Suddenly, the air became heavy, charged with an unbearable barometric pressure. From the crater hidden in the thickness of the ridge, a massive exhalation of vapor from Krakatoa erupted into the atmosphere, a volcanic breath that dragged with it a low-frequency wave. The impact was physical: a subsonic vibration that struck the children’s chests like an invisible hammer, emptying their lungs and making the air in their throats taste of molten iron. The entire mountain seemed to collapse upon their ears, a dull roar that was not heard, but felt in the bone structure of their skeletons, forcing them to buckle their knees so as not to be knocked down by the shockwave.

The subsonic vibration was not just an atmospheric event, but a master key for the machinery they carried. In direct response to the mechanical impact of the sound, the internal gears of the Aether Seismograph inside the bag began to protest. The device, which until then had remained in a state of inert rest, reacted to the frequency of the volcanic exhalation. The finely calibrated bronze dials unlocked with a dry, metallic click, initiating a slow and methodical reverse rotation upon their notches, as if the artifact were rewinding the time of geology, undoing the knots of tension accumulated over centuries.

The rotation of the dials was not a simple mechanical movement, but a phase-adjustment process. As the notches moved, the artifact emitted a high-pitched hum that resonated in Nilo’s teeth. The reverse rotation seemed to extract energy from the volcanic vapor, reconverting the chaos of the roar into a coherent wave frequency. It was a self-learning feedback mechanism: faced with the brute force of nature, the artifact responded with the precision of a machine that not only calculated the movement of the earth but translated it into a language the bismuth could digest, preparing the terrain for the next phase of their mapping.

In the midst of that unlocking process, Nilo felt a sudden void in his connection to the artifact and, consequently, to Runa. Moving barely a meter away due to the force of the wave, the bioelectric circuit that united them fragmented. The gears of the Aether Seismograph, which mere seconds before were spinning with fluidity, froze instantly with an agonizing screech, as if time had stopped inside the brass casing. Nilo then understood the nature of his condemnation: their physical proximity was not a choice, but an indispensable technical requirement. Their biomagnetic fields, intertwined with the artifact, functioned as the necessary conductive bridge so the machine would not collapse into a state of absolute rigidity.

Runa, sensing the urgency of the blockage, stepped back and placed herself shoulder to shoulder with Nilo, closing the space that separated them. As soon as their skin was within a few centimeters, the charge transfer was reestablished, and the gears of the Aether Seismograph began to spin again with renewed smoothness. It was a technical communion, a forced dependency where the distance of a meter represented the abyss between function and paralysis. The artifact demanded the unity of its observers; if they drifted apart, the bioelectric circuit was interrupted, depriving the mechanism of the vital energy necessary to maintain its inertia, turning the youths into mere containers of an inert weight that threatened to crush them.

This revelation transformed their march into a choreography of iron. They had to move as a single body, coupled by the need not to let the machine fall asleep, for the coldness of the immobilized gears was a threat to the integrity of their own nervous systems. If the gears froze, the energy trapped in the bismuth would seek an escape route, and that route, given their proximity, would be none other than the electrical network of their own bodies. Immobility was, therefore, a form of electromagnetic death. Each step forward had to be calculated based on the other's position, an embrace imposed by the physics of a technology that fed on their closeness.

As they advanced, the sky turned a yellowish, almost sickly color. The clouds, laden with chemical residues from the recent volcanic activity, began to discharge a precipitation of sulfuric acid rain. The drops, dense and corrosive, began to eat away at the surface of the basaltic rocks with a constant hiss, burning everything they touched with a haze of irritating vapor. Visibility was reduced to a few meters, and the air became so dense that breathing turned into an act of forced purification through their dampened handkerchiefs.

Seeking shelter against the corrosion of the rain, they located a deep hollow in a wall of basaltic rock that rose like a natural rampart. It was a dark niche, protected by an overhang of volcanic stone that diverted the acidic flow. They slid inside, feeling the temperature drop sharply within the cave. The space was narrow, barely enough for both, forcing them to remain in constant physical contact which, far from being uncomfortable, became the only guarantee of their survival against the paralysis of the gears.

Inside the hollow, protected by the basalt, they observed the dance of the artifact’s dials resting between them. The seismograph continued its reverse rotation, feeding on the electromagnetic energy released by the acid rain as it impacted the exterior slope. The niche became a trench, a garrison where linear time seemed to have stopped while they, the cartographers of the invisible, waited for the outside world to stabilize. In the dimness of the rock, the eyes of Runa and Nilo reflected the bluish glow of the alloy, a cold light that did not illuminate, but revealed the architecture of a reality that no longer belonged to them, a reality written in the remnants of matter.

The rain continued to lash the exterior with a monotonous rhythm, a drumming that seeped into the vibration of the basalt walls. They, huddled in their corner, remained in absolute silence, avoiding any movement that might interrupt the delicate energy bridge. They knew that upon leaving, the hill would await them with new challenges, new strata of static, and new industrial collisions that only their unity could resolve. The ascent was not over; they were only just beginning to understand that the summit was not a place, but a frequency, and that they, as observers, were the only living notes capable of playing the earth’s score.

capitulos.sinopsis: A dry electrical storm lashes the strait. A chassis pulse fries the components of Nilo's mobile speaker while testing the antenna.
texto: The horizon over the Strait of Saint Lawrence had mutated into a strip of oxidized lead, a tension line where the atmosphere seemed to have reached the limit of its own capacitance. A dry electrical storm, fueled by the static suspension of volcanic dust particles dragged from the deep strata by a thermal anomaly, began to discharge without the preamble of rain. There were no moisture-laden cumulonimbus clouds, only a dense, electrified haze that crackled with bluish voltaic arcs, leaping from rock to rock like whips of cold light desperately seeking a ground in the Earth's crust.

Each discharge was an acoustic detonation, a dry snap that reverberated against the basalt cliffs, turning the air into a hostile, ozone-charged medium. Static electricity made the hair on Runa and Nilo's arms stand on end, while the environment filled with the acrid smell of burnt metal and sulfur, a harbinger of a geological alteration that escaped any conventional meteorological framework. Lightning struck in fractal sequences, illuminating the landscape with a flickering light that stripped the scenery of its shadows, revealing the skeletal structure of a land that seemed to be waking from a thousand-year slumber under the influence of that artificial storm.

Nilo, his hands trembling from the static overload in the air, held the bimetallic artifact at the entrance to the cave that served as their precarious shelter. The piece, an amalgam of bismuth and crystallized coltan in the shape of an ancestral astrolabe, began to respond to the fury outside through an internal low-frequency vibration that transmitted through his bones. As the discharges struck the surface of the crag, the metal absorbed the mechanical and acoustic energy of the detonations, transforming the environmental violence into a rhythmic oscillation that made the object emit a faint, coppery glow, as if the bismuth core were igniting from within under infrared pressure.

The device did not merely reflect light; the astrolabe devoured the shock waves that bombarded the cave entrance, stabilizing them within its atomic structure. Nilo watched, mesmerized, as the small diamagnetic needles etched into the brass surface began to orbit on their axis, defying gravity and inertia. The humming that emanated from the object was not an auditory sound, but a planetary tinnitus that settled at the base of his skull, reminding him that the artifact was not a tool, but a sensory membrane connecting the cave to the magnetic pulse coursing through the tectonic plates of the strait under that burning sky.

In an excess of tactical imprudence, driven by a curiosity that ignored the risks of resonance, Nilo extracted his telephone device from his pocket, seeking a confirmation signal that might explain that chaos. The terminal, a jumble of modern circuits and printed antennas, looked like an obsolete toy in the face of the astrolabe. With fingers numbed by the electric cold, Nilo activated the short-range network, trying to tune the broadband spectrum to the rotation frequency of the metal object. His intention was merely experimental: he wanted to know if the local wireless network could influence the geometry of the artifact's rotation.

The effect was immediate and catastrophic. The instant the radio signal, a high-frequency carrier wave, collided with the concentrated magnetic field of the astrolabe, a destructive coupling occurred. The bismuth and coltan object, upon detecting the digital intrusion, reacted like a cornered creature, firing an inverted and concentrated electromagnetic pulse toward the source of the interference. There was no sonic boom, but a sudden implosion of energy that swept across the entire radio frequency spectrum in a radius of ten meters, instantly carbonizing the micro-components of Nilo's phone.

The phone's speaker emitted a high-pitched screech, an electronic agony that ended abruptly in absolute silence as the screen went black, traversed by a whitish crack caused by the thermal shock. The device's metal heated up until it nearly burned Nilo's palm, forcing him to drop it onto the volcanic rock of the cave floor. The hardware, now inert, had become a simple block of useless silicon and plastic; the pulse had fried every copper track on its motherboard, reducing months of data and communications to a memory of digital ash.

Runa, who had been observing the reaction from the shadows, approached to analyze the smoldering remains, her eyes scanning the geometry of the destruction with analytical coldness. Her explanation was technical, devoid of compassion: the artifact was not a receiver, but a massive transducer. She explained to her brother that the coupling had been inevitable; by trying to force a controlled radio signal against the astrolabe's inertial mass, it had returned the wave, multiplied exponentially by its atomic density. Nilo's digital technology simply did not possess the physical integrity to withstand such a pure and archaic frequency.

The difference, Runa pointed out, lay in the nature of the medium: Nilo's information was contained in ephemeral charge states, whereas the artifact stored energy in the very alignment of its coltan crystals. They had tried to converse with a geological entity using the volatile language of a society that confused speed with understanding. The astrolabe had not attacked; it had simply defended itself against an intrusion it considered background noise, an interference that threatened its resonant stability within the storm system.

After regaining her composure, Runa took out a notebook and began to record, in precise and almost military handwriting, the geometric patterns that the astrolabe's needles continued to trace in the air. She watched how the needles drew arcs that corresponded to variations in the intensity of the surrounding electromagnetic field, creating an abstract choreography that, according to Runa, constituted a language of invisible cartography. Each discharge of the storm caused a syncopation in the needles' rotation, a graphic record of the tectonic shifts they were now, finally, beginning to translate.

The notebook filled with vector diagrams and wave functions, an exhaustive chronicle of the physical tensions that the modern world preferred to ignore. Runa documented the "tinnitus" of the planet, that constant humming emerging from the depths that only the artifact was capable of filtering. For her, each turn of the needles was geological data, a coordinate on a resonance map that expanded beyond the physical borders of the library, encompassing the entirety of the seabed beneath the strait, where plates were sliding with a friction that began to generate its own electrical signature.

However, the immersion in that technical record was interrupted by a change in the atmospheric composition. The infiltration of sulfur dioxide, released by the volcanic fissures the storm had finished opening at the base of the cliff, began to fill the cave with a yellowish and suffocating mist. The air became unbearably dense, charged with a toxicity that irritated the mucous membranes and clouded their vision. With coordinated and hurried movements, the two siblings extracted their handkerchiefs, dampening them with water from their canteens and knotting them over their noses and mouths, protecting their airways from the chemical corrosion.

The gloom of the cave, now stained by the sulfuric haze, became a claustrophobic environment. Through the gas curtain, Nilo crawled toward the edge of the opening to try to regain perspective. The outside world looked distorted by thermal oscillations, a surreal scene where the earth and sky seemed to melt into a single unstable entity. Despite the danger, their instinct for observation remained intact, an almost reflexive response to the involuntary training to which they had been subjected since the astrolabe entered their lives.

In the distance, silhouetted against the electric brightness of the waves crashing violently against the rocks of the strait, appeared a mechanical silhouette. It was the HMS Charles Fourier, an iron-hulled steamship, plying the strait despite the extreme weather conditions. Its boilers spewed plumes of black smoke that mingled with the storm's discharges, creating a spectacle of industry and brute force. The flashes from its furnaces intermittently illuminated its structure, revealing the movement of connecting rods and pistons struggling against the current of the strait.

The ship looked like a living anachronism, a steam machine facing a storm that, in essence, was the electrical manifestation of a geological past the world had forgotten. Nilo and Runa watched as the vessel swayed with a heavy slowness, its metal resonating with the same shock waves that made their own astrolabe vibrate. The encounter was brief, but revelatory: that ship was not just a boat, it was a moving receiver, one more piece on the resonance map they were beginning to chart, a variable of steel in a planetary equation that, little by little, was starting to make sense in Runa's notebook.

capitulos.sinopsis: They find an abandoned colonial relay station, and Nilo constructs an improvised induction coil using copper filaments.
texto: The structure rose like a splintered stake on the flank of a hill forgotten by official cartography, a shack of charred wood and cedar boards twisted by the region's endemic humidity. It was a relay station for an optical and mechanical telegraph system, a vestige of the colonial era when information traveled via mirage and pulley, before the world was stitched together by submarine cables and fiber optics. The building, stripped of its precision lenses, bore the scars of a savage obsolescence: the zinc roof had partially collapsed, allowing the gray light of twilight to filter like a shroud over a floor littered with industrial detritus and metallic insect nests.

Runa ran her fingertips along the rotting grain of the walls, feeling the residual vibration of a mechanism that still seemed to remember its original purpose. There, in the ozone-heavy gloom, the silence was not an absence of noise, but a dense accumulation of data that the air attempted to process without success. The wood, impregnated with ancient oils and saltpeter, emanated a rancid aroma, the characteristic scent of things built to outlast their utility, now trapped in a historical paralysis that observers perceived as a dull hum at the base of the skull.

From the entrance, Runa watched how the light fragmented over the remains of the cast-iron gears. With a voice that seemed to shed its human warmth to adopt a technical cadence, she explained the metamorphosis of communication to Nilo: the telegraph was an extension of the human pulse, a binary sequence of sharp taps that mimicked the heartbeat, an architecture of dots and dashes where meaning resided in the deliberate interruption of silence. She contrasted this method with modern data modulation, an unfathomable tide of electromagnetic packets that sought not clarity, but perpetual redundancy, turning communication into an invisible spectrum that traversed bodies without finding an anchor point.

Nilo listened, though his eyes were fixed on the station's innards, searching for the missing link between primitive mechanics and the 5G saturation that had dragged them there. While Runa discoursed on the degradation of semantics in the information century, the boy knelt on the rickety floorboards, brushing away layers of grime accumulated over decades. His hands, stained by the soot of the Library's sub-basement, found a fragment of ancient copper cable, a thick, almost rigid filament that detached from a wall like the exposed nerve of a metal giant. That copper was not degraded by conventional rust, but instead retained an unnatural, almost magnetic ductility—the ideal material for converting atmospheric noise into usable mechanical work.

The precision with which Nilo handled the filament was almost surgical. With the pulse of someone who knows that reality is, in essence, an open circuit, he began to wrap the copper around the brass frame of the astrolabe they had carried from the Library. Each turn was a calculation of resistance and capacitance, an improvised induction coil seeking to channel the stray energy that floated in the environment. The metal, upon making contact with the copper, seemed to emit a low-frequency sound, a harmonic groan that resonated in the chests of both observers, as if the artifact itself were waking from a geological slumber to reclaim its function in the planetary network.
