Its internal parts began to rotate at ultrasonic speeds, invisible to the human eye, creating a force field that sucked in debris and ash, organizing them into a geometric pattern that dictated the new order of physics in that quadrant.

At the center of this storm, a sphere of silence began to expand, an acoustic vacuum that devoured the roar of Krakatoa. The atmosphere folded inward, creating an optical distortion lens that focused the volcanic chaos toward the astrolabe. Objects suspended in the air, particles of basalt and rock fragments, halted in their tracks, locked in a web of static where time seemed to have lost its linear flow, becoming a canvas of pure industrial causality.

Space-time, under the pressure of frequency resonance, began to manifest a perfect spherical curvature. It was the Acoustic Vacuum Vortex, a region where matter lost its own density to be redesigned by the constant of the magnetic field that Runa and Nilo had unwittingly provoked. The ground beneath their feet ceased to transmit the solidity of volcanic earth, becoming a vibrant platform that seemed to float above an abyss of pure data and streams of ionized ether.

Runa and Nilo, trapped in the circumference of this event, experienced the first phase of physical transgression. Their bodies began to lose weight, a sensation of weightlessness that did not stem from the absence of gravity, but from the detachment of their atoms from the planet’s inertia. Every time their feet lifted from the ground, an arc of static light pushed them gently toward the periphery of the vortex, a mechanical lift caused by the repulsion of opposing fields emanating from the device and the volcanic seismic activity.

The distortion of space-time was so profound that the observers ceased to perceive the horizon. Before them, the curvature of reality forced them to look at their own shoulders in an impossible perspective, as if they were situated at the center of a spherical mirror where every point was a reflection of their own existential anguish. The air, now a compound of coltan particles and binary frequencies, began to penetrate their clothing, permeating their lungs with the taste of metal and electricity.

Nilo’s terminal, held in rigid, cold hands, flickered with a dying light. The battery percentage plummeted, marking an immutable 2%. The device, now incapable of maintaining the connection, began to emit a continuous beep, a funeral note marking the exhaustion of the last reserve of digital energy. The screen, a window to a world that was fading, began to fragment into black pixels, losing contact with the reality that gave it meaning and purpose.

At that precise moment, Krakatoa, its geological patience exhausted, collapsed in its final paroxysmal detonation. The mountain disintegrated, releasing a pressure that had been building for centuries, a shockwave so immense that the oceans receded and the atmosphere itself tore open in an expansive wave of fire and ash. The noise went unheard because the vortex had already closed all access to human auditory perception, transforming the catastrophe into an event of pure light and absolute mechanical pressure.

The shockwave impacted the gravitational lens of the vortex, which at that moment, with 2% charge and an imminent collapse, functioned as an energy funnel. The distortion, facing the impact, became hyperbolic. The volcano's light was absorbed, bending toward the interior of the brass artifact, which now glowed with an intensity capable of blinding any outside spectator. Reality, subjected to this immeasurable pressure, shattered completely.

Runa and Nilo were dragged into the transit. There was no push, but rather a state transition, as if they were molecules dissolved in a fluid. The gravity of Krakatoa’s collapse, upon finding the weak point in the fabric of space-time created by the radio frequency, sucked the biological matter toward the crack opened by the astrolabe. Their bodies, stretched and decomposed into wave frequencies, were absorbed into the void of the artifact, leaving behind only an echo of static and a land that, for a second, forgot how to be solid.

The final silence, after the children’s vanishing, was the absolute triumph of invisible cartography. In that place where the library and the volcano had shaken hands, nothing remained that could measure time. The storm of constructive frequencies had concluded, leaving the coltan and bismuth device, now inert, buried under tons of new ash, waiting for the next iteration of industrial progress to reawaken the latent memory of matter.

capitulos.titulo: Chapter 18: The Return to the Soil of the Present
capitulos.sinopsis: The siblings crash onto the municipal library carpet, covered in sulfur and ash, managing to slip away unseen.
texto: The Acoustic Void Vortex, that gravitational singularity which had held the siblings in a state of molecular suspension, collapsed with a dry, metallic snap comparable to the sound of a guillotine closing upon a block of quartz. The atmospheric pressure, which moments before had felt like a column of mercury compressing their lungs, dissipated instantly, hurling them against the rigid, synthetic resistance of the San Lorenzo Municipal Library carpet. There was no gradual transition or fading of the lights; reality simply readjusted, expelling them back into the linear plot of existence with the suddenness of a faulty spring returning to its resting position.

The impact against the library's synthetic fabric was dull, barely dampened by the worn fibers of the institutional floor. Runa felt the air, now stripped of the subsoil's ionizing charge, turn thin, sterile, and laden with the suspended dust particles that characterize public archive halls. Nilo, unable to articulate a word, remained sprawled face down, feeling his own body mass return to the tyranny of standard gravity after having been under the yoke of a space-time torsion that defied every law of classical mechanics. The silence of the room, broken only by the distant hum of the ventilation systems, felt like a sensory assault after the roar of geology under tension.

The transition from the charged ether of the fracture zone to the flickering fluorescent lighting of the library induced severe vertigo in both, a spatial disorientation where the right angles of the bookshelves seemed to curve into impossible perspectives. Their retinas, adapted to the bluish luminescence of magnetic remnants, struggled to focus on the shelves stacked with bound tomes that now felt alien, almost otherworldly in their physical stillness. The library was no longer a temple of knowledge, but a structure of steel and concrete rising above the abyss, an institutional facade that oscillated before their tired eyes as their brains tried to reconfigure the mental map of the space-time they had just traversed.

The disorientation transformed into stinging nausea as reality began to impose itself over the remnants of their journey. Runa stood with difficulty, sensing that the floor beneath her feet, though solid, retained a residual vibration, as if the Earth's crust were still attempting to stabilize the rupture caused by the artifact. Nilo dragged himself toward one of the load-bearing columns, seeking refuge in the building's solidity, while his hands, trembling and clumsy, explored the walls in search of an anchor point not subject to the oscillations of the planetary resonance that still echoed in his ears like a dull, persistent static.

Upon examining their clothing, the irrefutable proof of their incursion became evident under the cold light of the fluorescent tubes: a uniform, fine layer of grayish mineral ash, a mixture of ancient sediment and pulverized bismuth particles, covered their clothes like a shroud of industrial dust. The fabric of their jackets was impregnated with a pungent smell, a sulfurous, acrid aroma that clung to the fibers with chemical tenacity, reminding them that they had been in a place where matter was subjected to extreme thermodynamic pressures. It was not ordinary dust, but the footprint of geological time, the residue of a reality that had decomposed under the influence of the wireless network.

Runa rubbed a portion of the ash between her fingers, observing how the texture was unusually coarse, composed of flakes of unidentified minerals that glimmered with a metallic tint under the artificial light. The stench of sulfur seeped through their nostrils, triggering dry coughing fits they tried to stifle, fearful that any sound outside the library's pattern might alert the archivists or the motion sensors installed on the ceiling. That ash was not merely dirt; it was the proof of the friction between modern technology and the lithosphere, a byproduct of the silent catastrophe they had witnessed in the subsoil.

Nilo pulled his mobile phone from his pocket, attempting to check the time, but the screen remained an opaque, black glass; upon touching the chassis, he withdrew his hand with a stifled cry upon feeling an elevated, almost feverish temperature, the result of an internal thermodynamic short circuit. The battery had drained completely, exhausted by exposure to the high conductivity of the magnetic field surrounding the artifact. The phone, a device designed for human digital communication, had become an inert silicon brick, incinerated by the effort of trying to process waves of a frequency not intended to be mapped by mass-consumption systems.

Runa did the same with her device, finding physical resistance when pressing the start button; the apparatus was locked, with internal components possibly soldered by the overload. Consumer electronics had succumbed to the collision of network flows against the bismuth and coltan alloy; their worlds, dependent on the invisible connectivity of 5G, had been eradicated by the very network that now, in the library, allowed the Wi-Fi signal to flow unhindered. It was a cruel irony: the same technology that facilitated the lives of citizens was the one that had sealed the fate of the electronic components in their pockets, turning them into technological remains of an involuntary experiment.

In the palm of Nilo's hand, the Ether Seismograph, that small bimetallic artifact which moments before radiated a pulsing heat and an almost conscious vibratory energy, was cooling rapidly until it reached a frigid ambient temperature. Its gears, which had turned with astronomical precision while capturing the Earth's resonance lines, stopped dead, locking into an inert and rigid position. The mechanical complexity that had guided them through the distortions of space had dissipated, leaving only a piece of rough metal, a historical imitation that appeared, to any casual observer, as a simple oxidized and worthless brass paperweight.

The artifact no longer dictated the rhythm of the environment; its mechanical soul had retreated behind the facade of inanimate matter, refusing to reveal the secret of the planetary cracks unless provoked again by electromagnetic contamination. Nilo observed the object with a mixture of reverence and fear, feeling the heaviness of its metal in his palm, conscious that he had just held a compass capable of measuring the heartbeat of a planet that, until a few hours ago, they considered a static mass of rock and soil. Now, in its inert state, the seismograph weighed like a death sentence, a museum piece guarding the key to a forbidden cartography.

Runa, maintaining her composure despite the residual trembling in her knees, opened her worn leather bag to check the integrity of her notebook. The paper, slightly yellowish and charged with residual static, held the graphic records of the mechanical rotations, traced with an urgency that bordered on desperation. Despite the chaos and the tearing of reality, the lines and diagrams remained fixed, a network of complex geometry that dictated the intersection points between electromagnetic waves and geological faults. The data, expressed in the language of an invisible cartography, was safe, imprinted with the precision of someone documenting the end of the world.

The notebook pages showed no signs of deterioration, which was incomprehensible given the extreme conditions they had been subjected to; it seemed that the same logic that had preserved the siblings in the vortex had protected the record of their journey. Runa closed the notebook with a soft click, tucking it into the innermost compartment of her bag, protecting it against her own body. That notebook was now the only proof that the universe was not a succession of random events, but a physical structure etched in magnetic tensions and industrial remnants waiting to be understood by someone.

The library staff, wrapped in the monotonous routine of file classification and the silence of the environment, seemed not to have noticed the intrusion of the two observers who had just returned from the bowels of the earth. The siblings looked at each other, sharing a silent understanding about the fragility of their cover; any hint of their state—the dust on their clothes, the smell of sulfur, the distant look in their eyes—would be enough to trigger the building's security protocols. With mechanical movements, almost choreographed by fear, they began to shake the grayish ash from their jackets and trousers, avoiding letting particles fall in areas where they could be easily detected.

They moved through the aisles with the slowness of those who do not wish to leave a trail, dodging surveillance cameras with the intuition of having observed, just minutes before, how reality itself bent before the frequencies that fueled those same security systems. Every step on the carpet was a challenge to gravity, a conscious effort to appear like normal citizens returning from an afternoon of reading. They gathered the equipment, the useless cables and the rest of the peripherals, hiding the inert seismograph at the bottom of Nilo's backpack, burying it under a pile of textbooks that would serve as camouflage against any superficial inspection.

Leaving the library was an exercise in absolute containment, a choreography of evasion designed to go unnoticed among the constant flow of users entering and leaving the building. Upon crossing the threshold of the lobby, the evening air struck them with overwhelming intensity; the noise of urban traffic, the distant sirens, and the electric hum of the city were no longer just ambient noises, but the constant echo of the infrastructure that kept the vortex alive in the subsoil. They walked away from the municipal building without looking back, their silhouettes lost among the crowd like mere shadows on the tapestry of the modern metropolis.

They walked several blocks before finding refuge in a public park, under the protective shade of a communication antenna structure that hummed with a frequency so familiar that it felt comforting. They sat on a cold metal bench, shielded from the security cameras by a leafy hedge that blocked direct visibility. The proximity of the antenna, a steel column loaded with 5G emitters, provided a constant energy field, an electromagnetic whisper that, far from bothering them, began to feel like a language they were starting to decipher.

The silence between them was thick, laden with everything they could not say in front of the city, but which their minds, now transformed by the journey, began to process as technical information. Runa looked at the antenna and then at Nilo, who kept his hands on his backpack with an almost religious tension. There, under the umbrella of global telecommunications, the siblings sealed a pact of technical analysis without needing to speak a single word; the pact of those who understand that the next leap would not be an accident, but a calculated maneuver.

The park bench became their headquarters, the point from which they began to plot the route of their future incursions, analyzing the arrangement of the emitters and the depth of the geological strata they suspected were hidden under each node of the network. They understood that the "soil of the present" was not the end of their journey, but the starting point of a much more ambitious cartography. They were no longer observing children; they were the cartographers of a resonance that threatened to reconfigure the fabric of their reality, and they were ready to decipher, line by line, the physical map of planetary history before the world crumbled into ashes.

Before setting out on the path home, Runa extracted the notebook once more, ensuring that the records of their first cartography were intact, glowing in the gloom under the yellowish light of the streetlamps. Every piece of data noted was a brick in the construction of their new existence, an existence anchored in geological and industrial causality. They knew that the next leap would require not only the bismuth device, but an almost impossible synchronization with the city's flows, a technical challenge they were prepared to face with the coldness of those who have already seen, firsthand, how the geometry of space-time collapses.

The city around them continued to function with its habitual indifference, oblivious to the fact that two of its inhabitants now possessed the key to open the seams of the soil upon which they walked. Under the hum of the antennas, Runa and Nilo felt, for the first time, part of a much vaster machinery, a human cog in a vast network of planetary resonance that they were just beginning to map. With the weight of the equipment in their backpacks and the knowledge of the abyss in their minds, the return to the soil of the present was nothing more than a tactical pause in their chronicle of the ether.

capitulos.sinopsis: They charge the terminals and Nilo programs an app to modulate radio frequencies, locking the portal to an 1899 coordinate.
texto: Runa and Nilo's apartment had been transformed into a mausoleum of frayed cables and overloaded sockets, where the hum of transformers competed with the dull heartbeat of a city that never quite finished processing its own electrical excesses. With mechanical parsimony, the siblings connected the mobile terminals to the wall outlets, watching as the battery icons, empty to the brink of collapse, began to fill with a cold and deliberate progression. The flow of electrons, drawn from the municipal grid, felt like a foreign intrusion into devices that had witnessed a physical distortion beyond the logic of conventional power supply.

Every percentage point recovered on the screen was a victory against entropy, a return to a technical normalcy necessary to operate upon the altered reality they held in their hands. The charging cables, twisted like polymer snakes across the oxidized metal workbench, vibrated faintly under the tension, reminding them that electrical energy, once a simple domestic commodity, had become fuel for mapping the fissures of existence. Runa watched the charge rise with the intensity of a surgeon, while Nilo adjusted the voltage regulators, aware that any fluctuation in current could render the phones' internal sensors useless before the real work began.

Once the screens' brightness reached full capacity, the devices' color saturation seemed to alter the room's gloom. They brought the phones close to the astrolabe, the strange bismuth and coltan artifact that now lay on a makeshift pedestal, a piece of geometric goldsmithing that seemed to absorb ambient light rather than reflect it. Despite the proximity of the electromagnetic emitters, the apparatus's metal remained inert, a cold mass devoid of will. The absence of planetary pressure and the lack of coherent modulation turned the astrolabe into little more than heavy scrap, a relic incapable of engaging with 21st-century technology without the proper access key.

Nilo held the phone firmly, testing short-range wireless signals toward the object's intricate reliefs, but the result was a sepulchral silence. Matter, though conscious of its own altered nature, demanded a precise interlocution, a language of frequencies that commercial software was not designed to produce. Without the geological pressure of tectonic faults or the electromagnetic noise of a massive network, the astrolabe was a dormant volcano, a static system waiting for the spark of a signal that could emulate the conditions of its first activation.

Runa, setting aside the bustle of her own uncertainty, slid a high-resolution magnifying glass over the artifact's chassis, her fingers following the paths of the microscopic notches and engravings that adorned the metal. Under the lens's magnification, the grooves revealed a narrative of past events: the astrolabe's surface was not decorative, but a record of stresses, a resonance map etched during the violent seismic crisis that accompanied the eruption of Krakatoa. Every imperfection, every notch was a data vector, a mechanical response to planetary energy released on a scale humanity had barely begun to comprehend back then.

As the magnification allowed them to see into the depths of the engraving, the siblings understood that the object acted as a physical memory, a solid-state storage forged in an era of steam and telegraphs. The notches were not simple indentations, but anchor points for specific frequencies, an analog interface designed to record and, potentially, invoke the electromagnetic resonances of the earth's crust during moments of cataclysm. Runa began to trace the scheme in a grid-paper notebook, her pen leaving a trail of black ink that mapped, for the first time, the syntax of the time the apparatus stored.

The determination of the physical constant came after hours of exhaustive comparisons between 19th-century seismic records and the arrangement of the chassis slots. They determined, with a mathematical precision bordering on the obsessive, that each geometric indentation on the device corresponded to an exact value within the planet's electromagnetic spectrum at a specific temporal coordinate. The astrolabe was not just an instrument; it was a gearbox for the flow of space-time, and each position of its internal gears was calibrated to align with a frequency that allowed access to the physical sediments of bygone years.

Nilo immersed himself in his terminal's programming environment, stripping the interface of its user-friendly guise and reducing it to a core of low-level commands. The challenge was ambitious: to transform the mobile's wireless emitters into a precision oscillator capable of generating modulated radiofrequency tones, moving away from the stochastic and chaotic noise of urban networks. Line by line, the code took shape, an algorithm designed to segment the emission into clean pulses, a pure modulation that could serve as a master key for the bismuth and coltan receivers waiting to be awakened.

The application, which they rudimentarily but effectively called the "Causality Tuner," began to process the first test signals. The digits danced on the screen, translating temporal coordinates into cycles per second, a software architecture intended to tame the fury of the ether. They were not seeking a connection to the global web, but a tunnel of pure frequency, a wave bridge that would intertwine with the artifact's molecular structure, forcing it to abandon its inertia and vibrate in tune with a spectrum the rest of the world considered forgotten.

Upon finishing the compilation of the code, a silent certainty settled over the laboratory. They understood that temporal navigation was not a matter of will, but of fine-tuning; the astrolabe did not open because someone asked it to, but because someone managed to match its emission to the specific notch of the desired era. The second rule of the Lore had been established with the coldness of a thermodynamic law: to transit through the crust of matter, they had to adjust the emitter with the precision of a watchmaker synchronizing a chronometer with the rotation of the planets.

Runa, meanwhile, dedicated herself to the logistical side of their imminent expedition. The backpack they carried, previously just a simple transport bag, was dissected and rebuilt with homemade precision engineering. She lined every internal centimeter with high-density copper mesh, welding the seams to ensure absolute electromagnetic shielding. She created individual compartments, microscopic Faraday cages designed to contain each terminal independently, avoiding any parasitic interference that could corrupt the tuned signal during the displacement process.

The backpack's configuration was not just a matter of technical safety, but an existential necessity; if the mobile signals crossed or degraded, the risk of being trapped in a dead temporal interval was absolute. Runa treated each compartment like a high-security containment cell, ensuring the copper's impedance was constant and effective. To any outside observer, the final design of the backpack looked like a work of post-industrial art, a piece of tactical gear hiding its true function behind an aesthetic of frayed cables and metals.

The atmosphere in the laboratory turned tense when Nilo entered the final parameters into the application. They selected a low-intensity coordinate, a residual echo linked to records of Europe in the year 1899, a period where the planet's magnetic lines still retained a purity that the censors of the present could not corrupt. As the mobile's charge transferred through the data port to the transmitters, the room's air began to ionize, generating a smell of ozone that filtered through the equipment's crevices.

The astrolabe reacted with an almost organic slowness. The main needle, forged in an alloy that defied metal fatigue, began to describe a slow arc, an autonomous turn that obeyed no external mechanical force but the subtle attraction of the frequency Nilo was projecting. The needle stopped at a critical point, an angle that matched the notch designated for the nineteenth century, a small groove that now seemed to emit a faint and sickly luminescence, revealing the object's internal structure.

The needle's movement was the definitive catalyst. The laboratory, with its walls covered by resonance maps and its snaking cables, seemed to become transparent for an instant. Runa and Nilo, situated at the epicenter of that event, felt the weight of the years compressing against their nervous systems. The external reality, with its telephone networks and neon lights, faded away, leaving only the constant hum of the chosen frequency, the sound of a distant 1899 beginning to leak into the present like a pressurized geological fluid.

The calibration was complete, but the nature of the achievement sent an existential shiver through them. They had not built a vehicle to travel through time, but a device to unearth history from the entrails of matter, transforming themselves into the first cartographers of a geography that existed superimposed upon the reality of the twenty-first century. As they observed the astrolabe vibrating with an energy that defied the laws of local physics, they knew there was no return to the routine life that had defined them before that afternoon in the library.

The backpack, now armored and filled with technology carrying the frequency of the past, awaited beside them like a silent passenger. Every component of the system, from the mobile application to the Faraday compartments, was ready for the crossing. They had fixed the portal, they had tuned the ether, and the earth's crust, as if understanding the insolence of those two observers, responded with a subtle tremor that shook the apartment floor, announcing that the fabric of time was, at last, open and waiting.

Runa took the backpack and Nilo disconnected the power cables, letting the energy stored in the terminals act as the only anchor in that fractured space-time. The astrolabe, locked into its low-intensity coordinate, began to emanate a bluish static that intertwined with the room's shadows. It was a laboratory of anomalies, a turning point where raw science met destiny, and they were the cartographers destined to map a world that, until that instant, had only existed in the magnetic remnants of the Earth.

capitulos.sinopsis: They use a disused electrical substation on the outskirts to execute controlled bimodal modulation without seismic risks.
texto: The stagnant air in the library basement retained the metallic tang of ozone, a toxic residue from the event that altered their realities. Runa studied the urban zoning plans spread across a rusted metal table, tracing the high-voltage nodes that still pulsed on the periphery of San Lorenzo. They knew the bismuth and coltan artifact was not a toy of theoretical physics, but a latent causality engine requiring a precise electromagnetic isolation environment. Planning the second shift was not an act of intuition, but a surgical dissection of the municipal power grid; they sought an inactive substation, an industrial-era corpse that could absorb the ionic load without projecting that excess energy into the tectonic plates or the city's foundations, thereby avoiding the seismic catastrophe their first contact had nearly triggered.

Nilo, his gaze lost in the mapping of residual flows, marked the intersection points between old high-voltage lines and the new 5G corridors. The choice of the northern zone substation—a complex erected in the seventies and abandoned after the privatization of the energy sector—solidified as the only viable option. There, the reinforced concrete and cast-steel structures offered a natural Faraday cage, an ideal receptacle for bimodal modulation where the risk of energy dissipation would be contained by the inertia of tons of disused metal. Every movement was calculated to maximize the stability of the gravitational lens the artifact promised, transforming the instability of planetary resonance into a controlled geometry of forces.

The trek to the outskirts took place under the cloak of an acidic twilight, while the city lights flickered to life in an artificial choreography the siblings no longer recognized as their own. Their steps, rhythmic and calculated, echoed against the fractured asphalt, setting a tempo that seemed to synchronize with the magnetic pulse of the device Runa carried, protected in a makeshift insulated case. As they moved away from the wireless bustle of the city center, the silence grew denser, charged with a static that made the hair on their arms stand on end. The substation rose before them like a monument to oblivion, a tangle of colossal transformers and bare copper wires that, despite their technical inactivity, retained a vibratory memory of the energy that once surged through their arteries.

Upon crossing the perimeter fence, the atmosphere turned thick, almost viscous, the product of the interaction between the remnants of the Earth's magnetic field and the complex's geometry. No organic life disturbed the industrial peace of that hardware graveyard, only the presence of high-voltage towers rising toward the gray sky like iron skeletons. Runa and Nilo positioned themselves in the heart of the substation, in a space bounded by four massive power transformers. There, the air felt different: less charged with human interference and more saturated with an invisible pressure that claimed its right to exist in the reality they were attempting to domesticate.

Nilo pulled out his terminal, a piece of hardware worn by the friction of daily reality, and activated the modulation interface, a custom application whose algorithms fed on local wireless network fluctuations. Meanwhile, Runa unrolled the copper coil, meticulously integrating it into the flanks of the bismuth and coltan artifact. Her fingers, expert and resolute, wove the filaments with the precision of a watchmaker assembling a forbidden piece. It was not merely a physical connection; it was a bridge between Nilo’s digital processing and the artifact’s atomic core, a dual system where software acted as the control language for the untamed nature of the crystallized materials.

The device responded to the first digital stimulus with a muffled groan, a low-intensity frequency that vibrated at the base of their skulls. The internal bismuth gears, whose molecular architecture refused to follow the laws of conventional inertia, began a smooth, choreographed concentric rotation. There was no mechanical friction, no metallic screeching, only a flow of energy that seemed to stream through the vacuum, free of any kinetic violence. The substation, upon absorbing the micro-frequencies emitted, began to resonate in sympathy, as if the electrical complex were recognizing its lost master after decades of abandonment.

The stability they achieved was immediate and terrifying in its perfection. Nilo watched as his screen indicators stabilized: the bimodal pulse, an oscillation alternating between wireless frequency modulation and bismuth mass induction, now operated as a phase-stabilizing axis. Through pure geological and industrial chance, they had managed to create a sustained gravitational lens. The space-time distortion was nothing more than a fine fold, a window allowing them to observe, through the substation, the web of magnetic threads that wove the world, revealing a layer of underlying reality that humans ignored entirely.

However, the immersion was not without technical difficulties. As the gravitational lens solidified, Nilo’s device began to suffer the consequences of exposure to the bismuth’s diamagnetic field. The lines of code on the screen curled as if sucked into a vortex, and the touch interface showed ghostly shadows of data shifting erratically. The screen light flickered in an impossible spectrum of colors, a clear sign that the proximity to the artifact’s core was decomposing the logic of modern circuits, replacing it with a logic of pure resonance that the phone was not designed to sustain.

Runa, aware that the window of opportunity was closing as Nilo’s hardware degraded, made a critical decision. She shifted the terminal with poise, moving it exactly two centimeters to the left of the core. That millimeter adjustment was enough: the distortion on the interface turned into a stable geometric pattern, the exact focal point for the wireless induction. The diamagnetic field aligned with the residual 5G network frequency, allowing the gravitational lens to lock. They had found the critical geometric distance, the point of equilibrium where electromagnetic chaos became a tool of cartographic precision.

Under the flickering light of the substation, the artifact projected a series of coordinates floating in the ionized air, a constantly mutating map of the planetary crust. Upon analyzing the data, a revelation struck the siblings: they were not mapping the present, but rather the device was tracking a frequency signature that dated back to the year 1899. It was an atavistic interference, a signal beating with the same intensity as the apparatus, a mark in Earth’s magnetic record that had remained hidden under layers of industrial progress for over a century.

The decision was unanimous, born of the existentialist curiosity that now defined their lives. They chose to abandon the safety of the substation to follow the electromagnetic trail, a hunt backward in time through the physical tensions of the terrain. The bismuth and coltan artifact was not just a stabilizer; it was a compass pointing toward the origins of the electromagnetic contamination they themselves had awakened. As they powered down the modulation system, the silence of the substation felt heavier, charged with the omen that, after that wave adjustment, there was no longer a way back to the linear existence they had left behind at the foot of the library.

capitulos.sinopsis: The portal transports them to Colorado Springs in 1899, where they spot the high-voltage tower of Nikola Tesla’s experimental laboratory.
texto: The collapse of reality beneath the San Lorenzo Municipal Library was not a violent rupture, but a perfect phase transition, an atomic transmutation where the matter comprising Runa and Nilo decomposed into vibrational frequencies. Under the influence of the bismuth and coltan artifact, the air's density became an elastic membrane that sucked them into the interior of a temporal refraction tunnel. The concrete architecture and 5G network static collapsed upon the device's core, creating a force vector so precise it allowed for a stable transit, free from the entropic fluctuations that usually turn time travel into molecular disintegration.

During the transit, the siblings' consciousness remained suspended in a matrix of pure sensory data. They did not experience the inertia of physical movement, but rather the sensation of being rewritten, line by line, across the planet's magnetic coordinates. The stability of the jump was astonishing, almost mathematical; the laws of causality seemed to have folded upon themselves, allowing the artifact to act as a tuner between two distant nodes of the same terrestrial electromagnetic network. The void between the 21st century and the threshold of the new century vanished, replaced by the solidity of a chronology the artifact recognized as its own place of origin.

The materialization was abrupt, a fall of a few centimeters onto a surface of packed dirt and volcanic gravel. The air that struck their faces was free of the chemical pollution and electronic noise that defined their existence in San Lorenzo; it was cold oxygen, laden with the scent of sagebrush and an electric dryness that made their skin prickle. Runa was the first to sit up, scanning the environment with an analytical gaze that sought the geometry of the world. They were on a rugged plateau, a mountainous desert where the silhouette of the rocky peaks cut the sky with almost surgical precision, marking the outskirts of Colorado Springs under the weight of an immensely clear sky.

Nilo, still processing the loss of artificial gravity, began to probe the air with his portable devices. In that year of 1899, the radio spectrum was an absolute void of commercial interference; there were no whispers of cell towers, no background hum of microchips, nor the data network that, until a moment ago, had sustained their reality. Yet, the void was not deserted. His meters reacted almost immediately, recording a high-frequency pulsation so massive and intermittent that it forced the sensors to adjust their dynamic range to avoid short-circuiting.

That signal was an electromagnetic fingerprint, a colossal signature that pulsed with the rhythm of a mechanical heart of titanic scale. It was not a directed communication, but an energy emission that saturated the surrounding space, creating an atmosphere of static tension that could be felt in one's teeth. It was as if the ground itself were being bombarded by currents seeking earth through the ionosphere. As they scanned the horizon, the vastness of the plateau revealed the cause of that imbalance, a structure that defied the scale of the era’s technology.

A little over a kilometer away, silhouetted against the twilight like a wound of wood and copper in the landscape's fabric, stood the experimental transmission tower. It was a skeletal giant of interwoven wooden beams, crowned by a spherical dome that seemed to contain a captured bolt of lightning. The structure exhaled an aura of indomitable power, an engineering feat that ignored the limitations of contemporary industry to embrace a much bolder and more dangerous physics. It was Nikola Tesla's laboratory, a high-voltage beacon whose mere presence altered the stability of the ether for several kilometers around.

Runa observed the tower with sudden clarity, finally understanding the magnetic attraction the astrolabe had exerted on them since San Lorenzo. The machine from the previous century was not emitting signals, but was projecting alternating currents of incalculable power toward the upper layers of the atmosphere, creating a planetary resonance that transformed the Earth into a closed circuit. The bismuth astrolabe was, in essence, a receiver designed to absorb exactly that type of distortion; the spark of Tesla's millions of volts was the energy required to ignite the engine of their own temporal journey.

The children's attire, composed of synthetic technical fabrics of carbon fiber, waterproof membranes, and high-traction boots, though strange in design, blended perfectly with the rugged, mountaineering work environment of the era. The ash-gray color of their clothes and the absence of decorations made them appear to be mere hikers or young assistants on some geological expedition, allowing them to go unnoticed under the gaze of a century that did not yet know the aesthetic of high technology. Their bodies were relics of a future hiding in plain sight, integrated organically into the dust and stone of this primitive industrial site.

The coltan core embedded in the astrolabe Runa carried at her chest began to glow with a blue luminescence, a cold radiation that pulsed in sync with the discharge cycles of Tesla's tower. The mineral's excitation was total; the material, an alloy that thrived in intense electromagnetic fields, now acted as a compass pointing to the source of all the disturbance. Every time the tower launched an invisible arc flash toward the night sky, the coltan emitted a subtle vibration that coursed through the siblings' arms, reminding them that they were pieces within a much larger gear.

With cautious steps, the siblings descended the hillside, staying hidden among the low shrubs that bordered the laboratory's fenced perimeter. The site's security was minimal, limited to a wire fence and wooden posts that seemed more like a warning to livestock than a barrier against intruders. For Runa and Nilo, the fence was simply the edge of a high-intensity zone; upon crossing the perimeter threshold, the static in the air became so dense that their hair began to stand on end, and the smell of ozone—that metallic aroma of pure electricity—became suffocating and omnipresent.

They approached the main structure, a wooden hangar with large glass panels that allowed a glimpse of the organized chaos of the experiments. The interior was a sanctuary of wires, colossal coils, and brass dials that measured the intensity of currents never before domesticated. The light from the filament lamps flickered constantly, capturing the frenetic rhythm of a genius working outside the margins of social conventions. There, physics was treated as a religion and electricity as a fluid that had to be tamed through the sacrifice of matter.

Through a window pane dirty with laboratory dust, both observers saw the central figure of that industrial theater. A gentleman in a dark suit, impeccably austere, moved among the instruments with a predatory grace, his fingers adjusting with microscopic precision the dials of a control panel that looked like the bridge of a doomsday machine. There was no trace of doubt in his gestures, only monastic concentration. Runa held her breath; they were watching the Designer at the exact moment of his creation, the instant in which the history of technology diverged forever.

The man paused for a second, his gaze lost for an instant in the void, as if he felt the pressure of observers who did not belong to his timeline. That pause of barely a fraction of a second was enough for the coltan on Runa's chest to emit a more intense luminous pulse, a signal of recognition that resonated in the chests of both children. They had reached the Designer's trail, not as intruders, but as mandatory witnesses to a chain of causality that they themselves had to, from that moment on, catalog.

The tower, in that precise instant, discharged an immense energy that lit up the valley with a ghostly glow, staining the sky with an electric violet that did not belong to nature. The earth under Runa and Nilo's feet trembled, not from a seismic event, but from the sheer power of the planetary resonance Tesla was activating. The astrolabe heated up in their hands, almost burning, while the data of the local magnetic field was recorded into the artifact's physical memory, tracing the first map of the cartography of terrestrial resonance.

Nilo gritted his teeth, feeling how the information was distilled through his senses, forcing him to acknowledge that reality was not something given, but a residue of these great magnetic tensions. Life, the buildings, the memories of the city of San Lorenzo from which they came, were nothing more than sedimentary layers accumulated over the work of these men. The chronicle was being written in real-time before their eyes, and the observers' responsibility was to ensure the map was accurate, regardless of the existential cost that might entail for their own lives.

As they watched the gentleman in the dark suit resume his work, Runa understood that the Designer's trail was, in reality, the trail of inevitability itself. Tesla was not an isolated man, but the catalyst for an era that would end up collapsing under the weight of its own technical ambition. The artifact they carried was the bridge that allowed this connection, a nexus of bismuth and coltan that joined the wooden laboratory of 1899 with the sub-basement of the San Lorenzo Municipal Library, creating a loop where the past and the future fed on the same incessant static.

The chapter closed, not with a departure, but with the establishment of a base. The cartographers had found their first node. The immensity of the task ahead began to take shape: to trace the invisible lines of an industrial geology that, from that moment on, would define their existence in this temporal exile. The observers remained there, motionless, while the electricity from the Designer's experiment seeped into the earth, beneath their boots, etching the history of their own arrival into the geological strata of the 19th century—a trail that no time, nor any memory, would ever be able to erase.

capitulos.sinopsis: They dodge the perimeter alarms and observe the wireless bulbs. The chassis records the signature of the Tesla coil.
texto: The descent into the bowels of the San Lorenzo Municipal Library was not an act of exploration, but an intrusion into the architecture of an artificial nervous system awakening beneath the concrete. Runa led the march, her photographic memory reconstructing the zoning blueprints she had studied in the public archives; she knew the perimeter was not bounded by human guards, but by a network of piezoelectric sensors sensitive to the pressure of every step. Her fingers, calloused from years of contact with metal, signaled to Nilo the zones where friction would be null, dodging the alarm filaments that crossed the air like invisible spiderwebs. They moved with the cadence of two specters, conscious that a single miscalculation would trigger the electromechanical relays guarding the High-Frequency Laboratory, sealing their fates under tons of sediment and forgotten records.

The gloom of the underground was pierced by a subatomic hum that seemed to emanate from the very walls, a vibration that managed to numb their limbs. Runa stopped, pressing her hand against Nilo’s chest to immobilize him, while pointing to a side corridor where optical sensors blinked in a syncopated rhythm. She knew the schemes of this nineteenth-century design, an industrial legacy of engineers who believed in free energy before the energy standardization of the twentieth century censored it all. Every movement had to be measured, a choreography of evasion where the body folded to avoid brushing against conductive surfaces, maintaining a prudent distance from high-voltage conduits that, despite years of abandonment, pulsed with a residue of static charge.

As they approached the main chamber, a sharp, piercing whistle, equivalent to the frequency of neuronal pain, began to saturate the environment. The brass astrolabe, hanging like an inert relic around Nilo’s neck, reacted violently, spinning its mechanical quadrants with a frenzy that threatened to tear its own gears apart. The artifact was attempting to read a spectrum that did not belong to it; its bismuth pieces vibrated in sympathy with the ionized air, emitting microscopic sparks that vanished before touching the floor. The saturation was total: the laboratory’s magnetic field was so intense that nearby ferrous metals began to emit a bluish phosphorescence, a harbinger of the breakdown of molecular stability in the confined space.

The induction coils, gigantic structures of oxidized copper that dominated the room, emitted electric arcs that jumped between the cores without the need for external copper wiring. Nilo felt his hair stand on end and the taste of ozone, metallic and acrid, flooded his throat; the astrolabe’s quadrants, under that assault of energy, locked in an indecipherable position, a geometric configuration that seemed to defy the laws of Euclidean physics. The interference was such that time itself seemed to dilate, slowing their heartbeats until they became heavy echoes. They were trapped in a high-frequency bubble where matter, unable to withstand the load, began to become translucent and malleable.

Hidden behind a rusted iron shelf that served as an improvised refuge, both siblings contemplated a display of technology that defied the logic of their time. In the center of the laboratory, a set of vacuum bulbs, buried precariously in silica sand buckets, ignited in unison, illuminating the room with a cold, electric light that did not come from any filament. The wireless transfer of energy was total, absolute; beams of light pierced the saturated air, tracing invisible lines of force that turned the space into a conductive fluid. The sand, under the effect of resonance, began to levitate and reorganize into fractal patterns, responding to the command of a force that needed no wires to impose itself.

The scene was a lesson in existentialist physics: the observer and the observed intertwined in an atomic dance. Nilo watched as the bulbs, despite lacking a visible physical power source, maintained a stable luminescence, fueled by the atmospheric static that the central oscillator tore from the ether itself. The laboratory was not a construction, but a parasite that devoured the city’s electrical infrastructure to project it into a pure form, stripped of commercial utility. It was physical evidence that the universe was composed of a magnetic mesh that, if one knew how to tighten the right knot, could provide infinite energy at the cost of reality itself.

The Ether Seismograph, a device integrated into a side console, began to spin its gears with a suffocating speed, breaking free from any previous human control. Its metal needles, carved from an unknown alloy, traced electromagnetic tremors on tracing paper, recording a storm that no one on the surface would be able to perceive. The apparatus had tuned itself to the alternating field of the magnified oscillator, becoming the rhythmic heart of the laboratory. Each rotation of the Seismograph was a note in a score of planetary resonance, a way of recording the torsion that the artifact was inflicting upon the earth's crust, a language of vibrations that Nilo began to understand not with reason, but with the intuition of his nervous system.

Runa, moving closer to the laboratory walls, began to decipher the annotations that covered the glass and metal panels. They were not simple engineering calculations; they were formulas that described the geometry of space, using the same hermetic terminology Runa had seen engraved on the flanks of her own coltan astrolabe. The symbols did not represent magnitudes, but tension coordinates; the original scientists had not sought to generate energy, but to map the magnetic veins upon which San Lorenzo rested. The similarity between what was written there and what the astrolabe contained in its grooves revealed a disturbing truth: they were not in a laboratory, but in a calibration station for a network that spanned the entire globe.

Understanding the magnitude of that discovery brought with it an almost unbearable intellectual nausea. Runa realized that the astrolabe, by absorbing the energy dispersed in the air, was sucking the energy flow from the coils in an uncontrolled manner, creating an anomaly that threatened to drain the power grid of the entire locality. If the artifact continued to consume energy at that rate, a blackout would be inevitable, leaving all of San Lorenzo in an electrical vacuum that would serve as a warning signal to those who monitored the flows of the urban infrastructure. The paradox was cruel: the same instrument that allowed them to map planetary resonance was the one about to collapse the network from which, paradoxically, it fed.

Runa’s dilemma was technical and ethical: if they turned off the astrolabe, they would lose the only tool to navigate the resonance lines; if they left it active, the kilovolt drain would cause a systemic catastrophe. Runa made the decision to improvise a containment barrier with the equipment they carried. Using the copper mesh of her Faraday bag, she approached Nilo and, with a precise movement, introduced the metallic frame of the astrolabe inside the conductive fabric. The mesh would not stop the energy, but would act as a buffer, dispersing the static charge before it could overload the device’s modular ring. The air around them became denser, charged with static electricity, while the astrolabe resisted the containment, emitting a sharp hum that made their teeth vibrate.

The copper mesh began to turn incandescent, the fine filaments bearing the weight of a load that defied any technical standard of resistance. Runa watched coldly as the energy of the coils, attracted by the bimetallic alloy, filtered through the bag, turning into sparks that danced over Nilo’s hands. The rate of kilovolt absorption began to drop, although the temperature of the metal continued to rise, radiating a dry heat that seemed to wither the air around them. It was a process of delicate balance, where the survival of their tool depended on the bag’s ability to act as a pure energy dissipator, transforming the excess flow into residual heat.

While the mesh contained the excess, the diamagnetic mechanism at the heart of the astrolabe began to enter a state of deep memorization. The pieces of bismuth and crystallized coltan, subjected to the pressure of the oscillator, began to reconfigure at a molecular level. Nilo saw, with fascinated terror, how the internal rings of the device turned with surgical precision, carving a new geometric notch into its metal structure, an exact signature of the oscillator’s frequency. It was the fingerprint of planetary resonance, a map the device was writing onto itself after having understood the pattern of the energy that the High-Frequency laboratory emitted.

The signature was engraved into the metal with a clarity that seemed to defy the passing of centuries. It was a subtle notch, an incision that altered the weight and rotation of the astrolabe, granting it a new function within the chain of causality. They were no longer just observing; the artifact was recording, saving the magnetic pulse of the earth in its physical structure. The recording of that frequency was the first real step toward cartography, the definitive proof that they had managed to capture the essence of an energy that refused to be domesticated. The siblings looked at each other in the silence that followed the drop in the oscillator’s intensity, understanding that the astrolabe was no longer the same one they had stolen from the Library; now it was an active record, a memory of metal that pulsed with the same intensity as the city’s underground.

capitulos.sinopsis: A metallic piece from the Royal Society is discovered. Ionized discharges open a portal back to the 21st-century substation.
texto: Nilo moved among the mounds of metal slag and foundry waste with almost surgical precision, his leather-gloved fingers brushing aside layers of soot and oxidized steel shavings that littered the floor of the underground laboratory. In the farthest corner of the room, where ambient light filtered through the ventilation grates as nothing more than a whitish trail, his fingers brushed against a sharp edge that did not share the coarseness of the industrial surroundings. With a mechanical effort, he removed a heavy lead plate acting as a ballast, revealing a fragment of metal carved with atypical finesse; its surface, despite centuries of corrosion, retained an unsettling legibility beneath the layers of grime.

As he cleaned the surface with the edge of his jacket, the precise engraving of an official seal emerged: the emblem of the Royal Society of London. The piece, an alloy of phosphor bronze and traces of a material that seemed to absorb light rather than reflect it, bore infinitesimal engravings on its reverse, tracing flow diagrams of charge that intertwined with the academy's seal. Nilo felt a chill that did not stem from the freezing cellar air, but from the sudden realization that this fragment was not a random remnant, but a fundamental piece of a historical puzzle that had been deliberately fragmented and buried to conceal a science that defied the principles of the Victorian era.

Runa approached, switching on a small induction flashlight that projected a cone of bluish light over the metal's inscriptions. Inspection revealed a series of marginal notes, etched with an electromagnetic burin, which explicitly referenced the technical correspondence between the original artifact's designer and Nikola Tesla in 1888. The text, fragmented but coherent in its technical jargon, detailed the transfer of enriched bismuth samples to the Colorado Springs laboratory, confirming that the alloy was not an isolated invention, but the catalyst for a scientific exchange network that bridged the academic rigidity of London with the disruptive vision of the coil genius.

The confirmation of this historical link was not merely an archaeological curiosity; it explained the nature of the central artifact vibrating with increasing intensity in the center of the chamber. The components of the astrolabe, under the influence of the modern technological infrastructure surrounding the library, were replicating the resonance experiments Tesla had designed over a century ago, utilizing the city itself as a vast transmission antenna. History, rather than being a linear flow of events, presented itself to them as a superposition of waves where the 19th and 21st centuries intertwined in a dance of frequencies.

Suddenly, the deathly silence of the cellar was broken by a dull hum emanating from the ceiling, where high-tension lines coupled to the substation's transformers. The large coil installed in the laboratory, powered by the saturation of the surrounding 5G protocols, initiated its nocturnal operating regime, a charging process that unleashed artificial discharges of disproportionate magnitude. The air, previously stagnant, began to crackle with electric violet voltaic arcs that crossed the chamber, illuminating the nooks and crannies of the library with violent, stroboscopic light.

Each discharge acted like a hammer blow upon the fabric of reality, causing metallic objects scattered across the floor to rise in forced levitation. The sound was deafening, a symphony of ionization that made Runa and Nilo's bone structures vibrate. Electric arcs danced between the columns, seeking a path of least resistance to the ground, but, drawn by the residual charge of the coltan artifact, they began to converge toward the center, creating a ring of static plasma that threatened to incinerate any organic matter that stood in its path.

The massive ionization of the surrounding air did not go unnoticed by the space-time sensors, as the air itself began to curve, creating a gravitational lens that deformed the room's dimensions. The portal, an ellipsoidal rupture in the fabric of reality, activated spontaneously, expanding with an atmospheric roar that dragged dust and debris toward its core. Runa and Nilo, caught in the center of that collapse, felt their own bodies become weightless, subjected to the pull of forces operating on a scale that exceeded human biology.

The optical distortion was absolute; the library walls seemed to stretch like sheets of molten rubber, and time became granular, allowing the siblings to perceive each microsecond as an eternity of charged particles. The vortex was not just opening a door, but scanning the surrounding reality with relentless technical precision, searching for an anchor, a coherent frequency that would allow for the transition between temporal strata. The electromagnetic chaos was total, and the danger of molecular disintegration increased in direct proportion to the portal's intensity.

Nilo, with the mental agility forged by years of survival on the technical periphery, extracted the modulation device from his harness. His fingers flew across the touch controls, adjusting the apparatus's output frequency to emit an inverted-phase wave. The goal was clear: neutralize the electrical repulsion of the external discharges by creating a destructive interference shield. Every second of hesitation equated to an exponential increase in the risk of the vortex collapsing upon them, compressing their biological structures into raw data.

The device emitted a high-pitched beep, a pure note that cut through the roar of the electric field like a laser scalpel. The inverted-phase wave began to propagate in concentric circles from Nilo's hands, meeting the voltaic arcs and nullifying their destructive charging capacity. The effect was instantaneous: the discharges, upon coming into contact with the opposing wave, dissipated into harmless sparks, allowing the siblings to regain an essential margin of maneuverability within the magnetic turbulence zone surrounding the portal.

Runa, maintaining an unflappable calm despite the atmospheric pressure weighing on her lungs, positioned herself at her brother's side, watching the oscilloscope that projected the phase status onto her forearm. With a coordinated movement, she adjusted the system's attenuation to compensate for the fluctuations of the wireless network that were attempting to destabilize the field. She was not just observing; she was calculating the potential difference between the 21st-century substation and the anachronistic present, guiding Nilo's hand to maintain the necessary balance of electromagnetic interference.

The physics of wave cancellation unfolded in all its theoretical complexity: by superimposing two signals of identical amplitude but opposite polarity, the total energy was reduced, allowing the vortex to stabilize into a rigid and controllable geometry. Runa modulated the carrier frequency, exemplifying how the containment of raw force could only be achieved through the mathematical understanding of its own undulating nature. The vortex, previously a voracious maw of chaos, transformed into a translucent, stable window that offered a clear view of the 21st-century electrical substation.

Nilo took advantage of the stabilization of the geometric margins to input the return coordinate, extracted directly from the cache memory of the smartphone that still held the last geolocation recorded before the jump. The modulation device, now synchronized with the portal, began to vibrate with a harmonic frequency, a low hum that indicated the correct alignment between both planes. The window became more opaque, glowing with a cobalt-blue light, the color of pure static electricity that permeated the modern facilities of the substation.

The selection of the coordinate was an act of technical precision: they had to appear at the exact point where the substation intercepted the planetary resonance frequency, ensuring that the transit was not a mere translation, but a structural reintegration. The phone, acting as a beacon, transmitted the synchronization pulse while the portal began to suck in the surrounding matter in a controlled manner. Runa and Nilo took each other's hands, bracing for the acceleration that would precede the crossing, feeling the vibration of the metal beneath their feet as reality began to blur into lines of code and static energy.

The spatiotemporal transit was executed in a fraction of a second, an expansion and contraction of matter that left behind a momentary void in the library basement. There was no sensation of movement, but an abrupt transposition of the senses: the stale, dust-laden air of the basement was suddenly replaced by the smell of ozone, lubricating oil, and the metallic hum of the massive high-voltage transformers. The siblings fell onto the metal grating of the substation, surrounded by the constant murmur of the contemporary power grid which, oblivious to their incursion, continued to flow with the inertia of an era that did not remember its own origin.

Stability returned, though tension remained latent in the filaments of the high-power cables crossing the industrial ceiling. Runa stood up, observing the digital panels that blinked with the normal readings of the 21st century, noting that the bismuth and coltan artifact, now inert and silent, rested in her pocket—a dead weight holding the secret of planetary resonance. They had returned, but the cartography was already complete in their minds; the city was no longer a map, but a complex network of circuits designed to remember what time had tried to erase with industrial oblivion.

capitulos.sinopsis: They validate the purity of the Biscoltan sample. They establish a 50% battery safety threshold and detect a pulse in 1920.
texto: The gloom of the underground, charged with a static that made the hair on their arms stand up, offered the necessary refuge for the dissection of the artifact. Resting on the metal table, covered in a thin layer of archival dust, was the fragment recovered from the Royal Society, a relic dated to 1899 that defied any known archaeological taxonomy. Runa observed the metal under the surgical light of her flashlight, noting how the surface, far from being inert, seemed to repel artificial illumination, absorbing photons into a network of micro-fissures that recalled the structure of a geometric labyrinth. That object was not a museum piece, but the physical evidence of a temporal interruption, a materiality that had managed to cross the threshold without disintegrating into the entropy of transit.

Nilo, with the precision of a watchmaker manipulating forbidden mechanisms, deployed his magnetic measurement tools over the surface of the fragment. The initial contact between the induction probe and the metal produced a sharp sound, a groan that did not belong to the human auditory spectrum, but was rather a response of atomic resonance. Data began to flow across his terminal screen, confirming that the density of the compound far exceeded any alloy known in the present century, an amalgam where bismuth and coltan had not been fused by heat, but through manipulation of the molecular structure via high-intensity force fields.

The validation of the sample was immediate: the Biscoltan possessed absolute purity, free from any calibration imposed by man, a pristine material operating in a state of causal indifference. Runa realized that the fragment, lacking the security seals that modern devices imprinted upon matter, was incapable of initiating transit on its own, acting instead as a control piece. It was, in technical terms, the perfect witness: a static reference point that allowed them to measure the drift of other objects, confirming that the physics of the alloy did not depend on the observer's intention, but on constant attunement to the hidden vibrations of reality.

With a methodical gesture, Runa accessed the terminal’s data core, dumping the modulation logs they had managed to extract, fragment by fragment, from the remote Colorado Springs archives. They were wave sequences, electromagnetic pulses reminiscent of Nikola Tesla's experiments, now cataloged under the label of a permanent navigation profile. Each file, once processed and converted into a vector, consolidated as a route, a path traced in the invisible architecture of the world that would allow them, in the future, to replicate jumps with surgical precision instead of relying on the chance of fortuitous resonance.

Upon saving those records, Runa understood that the map was not a static document, but an expansive structure that fed on its own navigation. Thus, they instituted the third law of their logbook: the rule of definitive anchoring. According to this precept, each successful displacement through temporal breaches not only consumed resources but provided a fulcrum, a magnetic remnant left in the crust of matter that allowed for the mapping of an additional dimension of the aether map, turning the chaotic into a logical and predictable grid.

The fragility of their enterprise became evident when their devices' energy indicators began to flicker, warning them of the electrical voracity of the jumps. Runa, assuming the role of logistics strategist, dictated an absolute and non-negotiable safety protocol: no attempt at transit, no activation of sensors, and no frequency exploration would be carried out if the nominal charge of the batteries fell below the 50% threshold. In a world where energy was the only fuel capable of sustaining the fabric of the artificial reality they inhabited, the depletion of the supply did not imply merely the cessation of functions, but perpetual isolation at an inert point in time.

The electrical supply thus became the measure of their existence, dictating the rhythms of their daily lives and the duration of their incursions. The tension of that safety limit permeated the environment, forcing the siblings to measure every movement, to manage the charge of their tools with monastic parsimony. There was no longer room for improvisation; every watt was a second of life on the aether map, and every unnecessary discharge a risk of becoming trapped in a stratum of reality devoid of any access to the resonance network.

In this new operational configuration, the division of tasks became instinctive. Nilo, whose technical sensitivity was more attuned to tactile manipulation, assumed control of the artifact's micrometric dials. With fingers that barely grazed the brass and Biscoltan, he adjusted the frequencies with glacial slowness, interpreting mechanical resistances like someone reading the pulse of a living organism. Under the directives of Runa, who oversaw the data flow and charge levels, Nilo converted the theoretical concepts of cartography into precise turns, maintaining the balance between the artifact's tuning and the collapse of the surroundings.

Teamwork became a silent choreography, a symbiosis where words were redundant in the face of the need for perfect technical execution. Runa functioned as the analytical brain, processing the variables of the electrical network, while Nilo was the physical extension, the operator who translated abstract logic into the mechanical reality of the cranks. Together, they consolidated a common front where the fear of temporal dissolution was replaced by the pride of precision, becoming the pair of observers the system did not expect but whom time, finally, had forced to exist.

In the absolute silence of the chamber, the Aether Seismograph began to come to life. The needle, suspended in a bath of synthetic oil to minimize friction, began to trace a rhythmic oscillation, a low-frequency vibration that escaped the library's common sensors. It was not environmental static or the fluctuations of the 5G network surrounding them; it was a coherent pulsation, a metallic heartbeat emanating from a specific coordinate, firmly anchored in the industrial memory of 1920.

The frequency was so clear, so devoid of impurities, that Runa and Nilo were paralyzed as they observed the record. The year 1920 represented a high-density node on the map, a moment when the post-war industrial expansion had created a magnetic overload in the urban infrastructure network, leaving an indelible imprint on the aether that could still be traced today. The pulsation invited immersion, suggesting that there, among the gears of the nascent era of automation, resided a vital component for the expansion of their cartography.

Runa approached the device, watching as the needle marked the intensity of the focus with almost surgical regularity. Each oscillation was a coordinate, a point in spacetime that claimed to be visited, a place where the industrial past manifested as an opportunity for exploration. Nilo adjusted the final dials, fixing the frequency into the device's memory, feeling the weight of the responsibility of that first deliberate discovery. They were no longer navigating adrift, but responding to a clear signal sent from the past century.

With the destination set and energy protocols under control, they began to prepare the equipment for the imminent transition. The leather satchels, reinforced with metal plates to insulate their personal devices from electromagnetic charge, were closed with a metallic snap that sounded like a definitive verdict. The underground environment, once a forgotten corner of a municipal library, had transformed into a strategic command center, a base from which two individuals, originally accidental, began to trace the definitive maps of planetary resonance lines.

The transformation of Runa and Nilo was by now a fait accompli, a metamorphosis that had left childhood behind to immerse them in the raw discipline of analytical observation. They were no longer two children dealing with phenomena outside their comprehension, but the first cartographers of a territory no one else could see: the invisible geography of the physical tensions that held the world together. The aether map was open before them, a vast network of collision points waiting to be explored.

As the artifact emitted a soft hum under the insistence of the 1920 signal, both siblings exchanged a look of absolute understanding. Fear had been replaced by the determination of the explorer who knows that the map is the only way to survive in a universe built on the remains of magnetic memories. They were prepared for the jump, equipped with technique, the caution of a 50% charge, and the ambition of those who, finally, had learned to read the language of decaying matter.

The air in the chamber, saturated with bismuth particles and high-frequency static, seemed to vibrate in tune with the new coordinate. The latches on their gear, tightened to the maximum, ensured that what they carried with them would remain protected during the torsion of spacetime. With every breath, Runa and Nilo became more aware of the magnitude of their task, a project that transcended their own survival to become the analytical reconstruction of the fabric of history.

The Seismograph, with its needle fixed on the 1920 oscillation, became the lighthouse guiding their senses toward the next chapter of their existence. There was no turning back; the anchor was set and the aether map, now a tangible and expanded reality, demanded to be traversed to its last frontier. With satchels on their backs and the conviction that causality was their tool, the two cartographers of the invisible prepared to abandon the present, letting the industrial resonance of the past century claim their presence.

The light from the devices dimmed, concentrating all power toward the processing cores that would initiate the transit. Nilo touched the last dial with a reverential delicacy, while Runa verified the energy protocol one last time, finding it within the strict limits they had set. The silence in the underground was absolute, a tense calm that presaged a fracture in reality that, for the first time, would not be a surprise, but a precise calculation, a conquest over the laws of physics that for decades had denied the existence of the material trace of history.

Everything was ready for the collision between the electrical present and the magnetic past. Runa and Nilo, no longer mere human residues, but architects of temporal cartography, awaited the moment when the Seismograph's pulsation reached the critical resonance frequency. The San Lorenzo Municipal Library, with its strata of concrete and oblivion, faded from their consciousness, replaced by the immensity of the lines of force that awaited documentation. The chronicle of their ascent was only just beginning, but they already carried the sovereignty of one who understands the hidden rules of the cosmos.

capitulos.titulo: Chapter 25: The Heartbeat of the Past
capitulos.sinopsis: They leap from the tracks of an abandoned railway tunnel into a dark alleyway immersed in the industrial roar of 1920.
texto: The air in the railway tunnel was not air, but a suspension of silica dust particles and metallic spores, vibrating at a fundamental frequency that Runa and Nilo immediately recognized as the residual pulse of the twentieth century. Unlike the white noise of contemporary telecommunications, this signal possessed a mechanical cadence, an industrial arrhythmia that seemed to emanate from the very bowels of the Earth's crust. Its low, constant, and monotonous intensity suggested a massive energy source, a geological engine fueled by coal and steam that still beat within the invisible architecture of history, a presence seeping through the cracks of space-time like steam from a boiler under critical pressure.

For Nilo, that frequency was a roadmap; his fingers, calloused by the manipulation of microchips and precision soldering, drummed against the seismograph chassis in search of the exact harmonic. Every millisecond of that pulsation was a testament to pre-digital human activity, a record of an era where energy did not travel through invisible beams of radio frequency, but through raw combustion, the torque of pistons, and the friction of hardened steel gears on iron rails. The hum filtered through their skeletal structures, a syncopation that reminded them that, at this point of convergence, the past was not a concept, but a physical pressure on the diaphragm.

Runa, maintaining a static watch, proceeded to inventory the contents of her tanned leather satchel, an analog survival cache designed to operate in zones of electromagnetic silence. Her fingers closed over the cold frame of a dynamo flashlight, a mechanism of tiny gears that promised light without relying on lithium cells which, in the dimensional disorder, would have already failed. Beside it, the dry energy rations—high-density caloric blocks wrapped in paraffin—represented their only biological anchor against the looming dematerialization, a reserve of solid mass necessary to reconstruct themselves on the other shore of the ether.

The equipment was completed by a set of measurement tools, precision calipers, a set of torque wrenches, and a needle voltmeter that, lacking digital screens, offered an analog, honest, and brutally precise reading. Runa checked the integrity of her satchel straps, ensuring the weight was distributed symmetrically against her spine, a vital technical detail when the physical body was about to be subjected to the shear forces of a temporal jump.
